### JanSz — 2016-02-05T18:48:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-188349>

**[International Society of Genetic Genealogy Wiki](http://isogg.org/wiki/Wiki_Welcome_Page)**
[http://isogg.org/wiki/Mitochondrial\_DNA\_tests](http://isogg.org/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA_tests)

**Mitochondrial DNA tests**
A **mitochondrial DNA test** (mtDNA test) traces a person's [matrilineal or mother-line ancestry](http://isogg.org/wiki/Matriline) using the DNA in his or her [mitochondria](http://isogg.org/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA). mtDNA is passed down by the mother unchanged, to all her children, both male and female. A mitochondrial DNA test, can therefore be taken by both men and women. If a perfect [match](http://isogg.org/wiki/Match) is found to another person's mtDNA test results, one may find a common ancestor in the other relative's (matrilineal) "information table". However, **because mtDNA mutations are very rare**, the match will not necessarily be within a genealogical time frame.

**Path of mtDNA transmission**
Males inherit mtDNA (shown in pink) from their mother but do not pass it on to their children. Males inherit Y-DNA (shown in blue) from their father. They pass on [Y-DNA](http://isogg.org/wiki/Y-chromosome) to their sons but not their daughters.

[](http://isogg.org/wiki/File:Male_DNA_Paths.png)

Females inherit mtDNA (shown in pink) from their mother. They pass on mtDNA to both their male and female children. Females do not inherit Y-DNA (shown in blue) from their father.

[](http://isogg.org/wiki/File:Female_DNA_Paths.png)

See also **[this animation](http://learn.genetics.utah.edu/content/chromosomes/typesmito/)** from Learn Genetics which provides a good explanation of the inheritance of mtDNA.

**What gets tested**
mtDNA by current conventions is divided into three regions. They are the coding region (00577-16023) and two hyper-variable regions (HVR1 \[16024-16569\], and HVR2 \[00001-00576\]). All test results are compared to the mtDNA of a European in haplogroup H2a2a. This early sample is known as the [Cambridge Reference Sequence](http://isogg.org/wiki/Cambridge_Reference_Sequence) (CRS). An updated reference sequence was subsequently published and samples are now compared to the revised Cambridge Reference Sequence (rCRS). A list of [single-nucleotide polymorphisms](http://isogg.org/wiki/Single-nucleotide_polymorphism) (SNPs) is returned. The relatively few "mutations" or "transitions" that are found are then reported simply as differences from the CRS, such as in the examples just below.

In 2012 Behar _et al_ proposed using an inferred ancestral reference sequence known as the Reconstructed Sapiens Reference Sequence.[\[1\]](http://isogg.org/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA_tests#cite_note-1) Bandelt _et al_ (2013) questioned the use of the RSRS and proposed the retention of the rCRS.[\[2\]](http://isogg.org/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA_tests#cite_note-2) Genetic genealogy companies have continued to report results in comparison with the rCRS. [Family Tree DNA](http://isogg.org/wiki/Family_Tree_DNA) report results in comparison with both the rCRS and the RSRS. The [Genographic Project](http://isogg.org/wiki/Genographic_Project) currently reports results in comparison with the RSRS.

The two most common mtDNA tests are a sequence of HVR1 and a sequence of both HVR1 and HVR2. Some mtDNA tests may only analyze a partial range in these regions. With falling prices the preferred option is now to test both HVR1 and HVR2. Family Tree DNA stopped offering the basic HVR1 test in May 2013.

Many people are now choosing to have the full mitochondrial genome sequenced (scientifically called the mtDNA genome or mitogenome). This is at times called the mtDNAFullSequence, the Mega Test, or the full mitochondrial sequence (FMS) test.[\[Footnote 1\]](http://isogg.org/wiki/Mitochondrial_DNA_tests#cite_note-3) An FMS test will report results for all 16,569 bases in the mtGenome and will provide the most detailed subclade assignment. An exact FMS match usually indicates a shared common ancestor within a genealogical timeframe.
[Family Tree DNA](http://isogg.org/wiki/Family_Tree_DNA) and GeneBase are the only two companies that currently offer the FMS test. A full sequence test was previously available from the now defunct company Argus Biosciences.

**Understanding test results**

The most basic of mtDNA tests will sequence hyper-variable Region 1 (HVR1). HVR1 nucleotides are numbered 16024-16569. Some test reports might omit the 16 prefix from HVR1 results, i.e. 519C and not 16519C.
Region HVR1 HVR2
Differences from rCRS 111T,223T,259T,290T,319A,362C Not Tested

More extensive tests will also sequence Hyper Variable Region 2 (HVR2). HVR2 nucleotides are numbered 00001-00576.
Region HVR1 HVR2
Differences from rCRS 111T,223T,259T,290T,319A,362C 073G,146C,153G

Family Tree DNA customers who have ordered a full mitochondrial sequence test from FTDNA can order a custom mtDNA report from ISOGG member Dr Ann Turner to help them understand their results.
mtDNA tree
Map of human migration out of Africa, according to mitochondrial DNA. The numbers represent thousands of years before present time. The blue line represents the area covered in ice or tundra during the last great ice age. The North Pole is at the centre. Africa, the centre of the start of the migration, is at the top left and South America is at the far right.

The most up-to-date version of the mtDNA tree can be viewed at [www.phylotree.org](http://www.phylotree.org). Phylotree is now on Build 16. Updates are released between two and four times a year. **As of January 2013** the different testing companies are using different versions of Phylotree. FTDNA is using Build 14, and the Genographic Project is using Build 13 or 14. FTDNA has indicated that they "have plans to upgrade to V15 as part of a coordinated effort with the National Geographic Genographic project".\[3\] **23andMe uses Phylotree Build 7.**

An up-to-date haplogroup assignment can be obtained from James Lick's mtHap utility. This tool is usually updated within 24 hours of the release of each new Phylotree build.

**Haplofind** is an alternative and less sophisticated tool more suited for batch processing mtDNA full sequence results. It does provide a list of **mutations** with reported and associated medical implications.

### JanSz — 2016-02-06T19:26:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-188419>

Jack at February 2016 webinar:
18:50
when sun rises in the morning there is no UV except on equator
it have only blue, red and green
blue and read are in balance
blue turns on anterior pituitary hormones, that starts circadian pace maker (red keeps it under control)
around 8:30-9AM uv light comes (in New Orlean), Chicago 10AM
UVA inactivates hormones that come to the surface
\---------------------------------------------------------------------
[http://patient.info/doctor/pineal-gland-and-circadian-rhythms#nav-0](http://patient.info/doctor/pineal-gland-and-circadian-rhythms#nav-0)
How is the circadian system synchronised with night and day?
**Sunlight (or light at 480 nm)** stimulates the RGCs to produce the photoreceptor melanopsin. Melanopsin 'signals' daytime to the SCN which in turn induces the pineal gland to suppress melatonin production.

RGCs → melanopsin → SCN → SCG → pineal gland → melatonin supression

In the absence of light or melanopsin, melatonin is produced by the pineal gland. In humans, it induces an almost irresistible urge to sleep. Its primary function is to signal day length to the SCN so that it can synchronise the day/night cycle with:

Endocrine rhythms
Body temperature
Glucose homeostasis
Lipogenesis
Locomotor activity

\---------------------------------------------------------------------
**[red](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Red) ~ 700–635 nm**
**[blue](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blue) ~ 490–450 nm**
\---------------------------------------------------------------------
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10100739](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10100739)

Effects of near-ultraviolet light **(UV-A; 325-390 nm, peak at 365 nm)** on the activity of the pineal serotonin N-acetyltransferase (NAT; a key regulatory enzyme in melatonin biosynthesis) were examined in chicks. Acute exposure of dark-adapted animals to **UV-A radiation produced a marked decline** in NAT activity of the pineal gland.
\---------------------------------------------------------------------

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-188428>

[http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/)

**Upload your data file for Haplogroup analysis:** (or use the [advanced options](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/advanced.php))

**Supported formats:** 23andMe raw data, deCODEme raw data, Differences to rCRS (mutation list), FASTA, GenBank Flat File Format, ASN1

**Help:**

-   [Frequently Asked Questions](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/FAQ.html)
-   [Frequently Asked Questions for Advanced Options](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/FAQ-advanced.html)
-   [Directions for 23andMe customers](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/23andMe.html)
-   [Directions for deCODEme customers](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/deCODEme.html)
-   [Directions for Differences to rCRS (FTDNA, et al.)](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/rCRSdiff.html)
-   [Directions for FASTA format (FTDNA, GenBank, et al.)](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/FASTA.html)
-   [Directions for GenBank Flat File Format](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/GENBANK-FLAT.html)
-   [Directions for ASN1 format](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/ASN1.html)

**Need more help?**

There is a [discussion about mthap on eng.molgen.org](http://eng.molgen.org/viewtopic.php?f=24&t=72). You can also email your questions to me at [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted]). So that I can best help you, please include a copy of the complete mthap report and/or your mtDNA data file in your email.
\===============================================
\===============================================
[http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/23andMe.html](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/23andMe.html)

**IMPORTANT NOTES:**

-   **23andMe raw data files downloaded prior to April 1, 2010 are known to have some incorrect results. The current version will automatically detect and filter out the inaccurate markers but this may exclude relevant markers and reduce accuracy. Please use a current data file for best results.**
-   **Please upload only your Mitochondrial DNA raw data file. Uploading the complete raw data file will work, but waste a lot of time and bandwidth.**
-   **Upload the txt data file. The zip file will not work.**

**Brief directions for 23andMe users:**

1.  Log into your 23andMe account. In the upper right corner click on "Account" and then select the "Browse Raw Data" option.
2.  On the "Browse Raw Data" screen at the upper right click on the "download raw data" option.
3.  Enter your password and secret answer.
4.  Select which profile to download data for.
5.  IMPORTANT: Select data set "Mitochondrial DNA." (All DNA file will work but take a long time!)
6.  Click "Download Data" button.
7.  Save the zip file somewhere on your computer.
8.  Open the zip file and extract the txt file inside.
9.  Click on the "Browse" button on [the mthap page](http://dna.jameslick.com/mthap/) and select the **txt** file. (The zip file will not work.)
10.  Click on "Upload."

\==============================================
\==============================================

### JanSz — 2016-02-06T21:22:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-188432>

@**[Josh Rosenthal](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?members/josh-rosenthal.15366/)**

(BACK)

mthap version 0.19a (2013-04-08); haplogroup data version PhyloTree Build 16 (2014-02-19)
raw data source genome\_Jan\_Szulc\_Full\_20140905084419.txt (23MB)

Found 2441 markers at 2440 positions **covering 14.7% of mtDNA**.

NOTICE: **You appear to have uploaded a 23andme v3 raw data file which has 9 known unreliable markers that will be excluded from this analysis.**

Markers found (shown as differences to rCRS):

HVR2: 263G 456T
CR: 709A 750G 1438G 4386C 4769G 8251A 8860G 15326G
HVR1: 16304G

IMPORTANT NOTE: The above marker list is almost certainly incomplete due to limitations of genotyping technology and is not comparable to mtDNA sequencing results. It should not be used with services or tools that expect sequencing results, such as mitosearch.

Best mtDNA Haplogroup Matches:

1) H5

Defining Markers for haplogroup H5:
HVR2: 263G 456T
CR: 750G 1438G 4769G 8860G 15326G
HVR1: 16304C

Marker path from rCRS to haplogroup H5 (plus extra markers):
H2a2a1(rCRS) ⇨ 263G ⇨ H2a2a ⇨ 8860G 15326G ⇨ H2a2 ⇨ 750G ⇨ H2a ⇨ 4769G ⇨ H2 ⇨ 1438G ⇨ H ⇨ 456T ⇨ H5'36 ⇨ 16304C ⇨ H5 ⇨ 709A 4386C 8251A

Good Match! Your results also had extra markers for this haplogroup:
Matches(7): 263G 456T 750G 1438G 4769G 8860G 15326G
Flips(1): 16304G
Extras(3): 709A 4386C 8251A

2) H5n

Defining Markers for haplogroup H5n:
HVR2: 263G 373G 456T
CR: 750G 1438G 4769G 8860G 15326G
HVR1: 16304C

**Here we provide a phylogenetic tree of global human mitochondrial DNA variation,** based on both coding- and control-region mutations, and including haplogroup nomenclature. This mtDNA tree is meant as a framework for evolutionary anthropologists, medical geneticists, genealogists and forensic geneticists. The tree is updated regularly to incorporate information from novel mitogenome sequences.

**How to create as good as possible artificial light.**
Artificial light is newer perfect, only sun is.

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)
@[Dia](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/11720/)
@[lilreddgirl](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/10648/)
@[Edgar Valenzuela](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/18106/)
\------------------
Dia said: ↑
I have 3 black lights and the solar glo that I use in my office. The red light that I use is just an ambient red that I use when it's dark outside. I also have the UFO 660, that I used occasionally and specifically when I do the D lamp.
\==========================================================================

Jack in Feb 2016 says
18:50
when sun rises in the morning there is no UV except on equator
it have only blue, red and green
blue and read are in balance
blue turns on anterior pituitary hormones, that starts circadian pace maker (red keeps it under control)
around 8:30-9AM uv light comes (in New Orlean), Chicago 10AM
UVA inactivates hormones that come to the surface
\==========================================================================
In trying to figure frequencies responsible I did found literature references to blue and UVA (so far nothing specific on red)
\------
[http://patient.info/doctor/pineal-gland-and-circadian-rhythms#nav-0](http://patient.info/doctor/pineal-gland-and-circadian-rhythms#nav-0)
How is the circadian system synchronised with night and day?
**Sunlight (or light at 480 nm)** stimulates the RGCs to produce the photoreceptor melanopsin. Melanopsin 'signals' daytime to the SCN which in turn induces the pineal gland to suppress melatonin production.
\-------
[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10100739](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/10100739)
(UV-A; 325-390 nm, peak at 365 nm)
UV-A radiation produced a marked decline in NAT activity of the pineal gland.
\============================================================================
red ~ 700–635 nm
blue ~ 490–450 nm
\============================================================================
As far as I can tell UFO660 is
Red/Blue (660 nm Red,455 nm Blue)
\============================================================================

**Conclusion:**
UFO lamp fits into lighting system when used at the beginning of the day for 1-2 hrs
next
reptile full spectrum light, Exo Terra Solar Glow 160W (few of this lamps)
until end of the day 10, 12 hrs latter.

But UFO 660 with its 455 nm Blue is not perfect fit, since my reference talks about 480 nm.
\=========================================

//

### JanSz — 2016-02-07T17:13:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-188477>

@[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/)

Boron deficiency causes the parathyroids to become overactive, releasing too much parathyroid hormone which raises the blood level of calcium by releasing calcium from bones and teeth. This then leads to osteoarthritis and other forms of arthritis, osteoporosis and tooth decay. With advancing age high blood levels of calcium lead to calcification of soft tissues causing muscle contractions and stiffness; calcification of endocrine glands, especially the pineal gland and the ovaries; arteriosclerosis, kidney stones, and calcification of the kidneys ultimately leading to kidney failure. Boron deficiency combined with magnesium deficiency is especially damaging to the bones and teeth.

Boron affects the metabolism of steroid hormones, and especially of sex hormones. It increases low testosterone levels in men and oestrogen levels in menopausal women. It also has a role in converting vitamin D to its active form, thus increasing calcium uptake and deposition into bone and teeth rather than causing soft tissue to calcify. Also other beneficial effects have been reported such as improvement of heart problems, vision, psoriasis, balance, memory and cognition.

As boron is essential for cell membranes and boron deficiency widespread, this may be an important cause for the initiation of tumour growth. Boron compounds have anti-tumour properties and are "potent anti-osteoporotic, anti-inflammatory, hypolipemic, anti-coagulant and anti-neoplastic agents" (1).

patients with rheumatoid arthritis commonly experienced a Herxheimer reaction and that this is always a good prognostic sign. They must persevere and in another 2 or 3 weeks the pain, swelling and stiffness will be gone. (4,5)

The Herxheimer reaction is an early aggravation of symptoms with increased pain. It is commonly due to toxins released by killed Candida and mycoplasma. This is very common with antimicrobial therapy, and borax definitely is an exceptionally good and strong fungicide. What surprises me, however, is that this fungicidal effect is already present at this rather low dose of 75 to 90 mg of borax.

One woman wrote in a recent Nexus Magazine of curing her Lupus and serious kidney disease in 4 month with **half a teaspoon of daily borax powder.**

Borax, similar to the equally endangered Lugol's iodine solution, can also be used to remove accumulated fluoride and heavy metals from the body (14). Fluoride not only causes bones to deteriorate, but also the pineal gland to calcify and the thyroid to become underactive. Borax reacts with fluoride ions to form boron fluorides which are then excreted in the urine.

[http://www.health-science-spirit.com/borax.htm](http://www.health-science-spirit.com/borax.htm)

> I tried about 2-3mg of MB yesterday and was wired/tired all night resulting in poor sleep. Could be something else as I often get nights like this however t got me thinking. I have several mutations (MAO-A R297R +/+, COMT V158M +/+, COMT H62H +/+) that all line up to mean I'm real bad at getting rid of neurotransmitters. This could be a big deal in my poor sleep in spite of the fact I'm doing a lot right in that area (pitch black room, good light cycles, magnetico, etc). MB is a MOAI which could be indicating that this is big on my sleep patterns.
>
> I know that circadian rhythm via BMAL and SIRT play a larger role in methylation and if cold isn't hormetic then the increased proton tunneling from that would also help massively but I don't have much room to improve these areas in my current situation so maybe I need to turn my attention to the micro scale of methylation.
>
> I know methylation supplementing is massively complicated as any supplement will affect everything else. Any one know if SAMe is generally any good with COMT?
>
> I took 1mg ish of MB this morning and had the same tired/wired feeling that's so uncomfortable and leads to worse palpitations. This got me thinking that these mutations could be one of the reasons palpitations were a HUGE problem for me a year ago. About 2hrs after I took a nicotine gum and it calmed me down a lot which is promising...
>
>
>
> Great post. Just wandering about how caffeine reduces serotonin syndrome. From wikipedia "[Enzymes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme) that regulate the [biosynthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biosynthesis) or [metabolism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metabolism) of dopamine such as [aromatic L-amino acid decarboxylase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aromatic_L-amino_acid_decarboxylase) or [DOPA decarboxylase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/DOPA_decarboxylase), [monoamine oxidase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Monoamine_oxidase) (MAO), and [catechol _O_\-methyl transferase](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catechol-O-methyl_transferase) (COMT) may be referred to as _dopaminergic_ as well". Seems to suggest, since you say caffeine is dopaminergic, that caffeine increases MAO however I have read that caffeine is a MAOI. Is caffine dopaminergic through COMT or L-amino acid decarbocylase instead? Does this offset the MAOI effects? Does caffeine help convert serotonin to dopamine???

I think caffeine is dopamine enhancing, thus

[http://www.nature.com/tp/journal/v5/n4/full/tp201546a.html](http://www.nature.com/tp/journal/v5/n4/full/tp201546a.html)
**Caffeine increases striatal dopamine D2/D3 receptor availability in the human brain**

[http://www.naturalnews.com/036412\_coffee\_dopamine\_caffeine.html](http://www.naturalnews.com/036412_coffee_dopamine_caffeine.html)
Caffeine is a naturally occurring chemical stimulant called trimethylxanthine. It can be addictive and debilitating as well as helpful, as both Bach and Beethoven, heavy coffee drinkers, would attest.
Caffeine stimulates the brain to produce the neurotransmitter _dopamine_ by occupying the brain's _adenosine_ receptors. Adenosine is what helps us feel like sleeping, but the adenosine receptors don't discriminate between adenosine and caffeine.

**The interactions of caffeine with monoamine oxidase.**
[Petzer A](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Petzer%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23850513)1, [Pienaar A](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Pienaar%20A%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23850513), [Petzer JP](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/?term=Petzer%20JP%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23850513).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23850513#)**
**Abstract**
**AIMS:**
Caffeine has been used as a scaffold for the design of inhibitors of monoamine oxidase (MAO) A and B. Substitution at the C8 position with a variety of moieties yields structures with high MAO inhibition potencies. Although the MAO inhibitory properties of numerous caffeine derivatives have been characterized, the possibility that caffeine inhibits the MAOs has not been investigated in detail. Based on the therapeutic applications and potential adverse effects of MAO inhibition, this study examines the interactions of caffeine with the MAOs.

**MAIN METHODS:**
Employing the recombinant human enzymes, the potencies by which caffeine inhibits the in vitro catalytic activities of the MAOs were recorded and expressed as the IC₅₀ and Ki values. The reversibility of inhibition was determined by measuring the recovery of enzyme activity after dialysis of enzyme-caffeine mixtures.

**KEY FINDINGS:**
Caffeine acts as a MAO inhibitor with Ki values of 0.70 mM and 3.83 mM for the inhibition of MAO-A and MAO-B, respectively. The results show that caffeine binds reversibly and competitively to both MAO enzymes.

**SIGNIFICANCE:**
Although structural modifications of caffeine lead to highly potent MAO inhibitors, caffeine is a weak inhibitor of MAO-A and MAO-B. At plasma concentrations (approximately 1-10 μM) achieved by normal human consumption, the MAO inhibitory potencies of caffeine are unlikely to be of pharmacological relevance in humans. The MAO inhibitory effects of caffeine should however be taken into consideration when using this drug in vitro and in tissue culture experiments where higher doses and concentrations of caffeine are often used.

This then leads to osteoarthritis and other forms of arthritis, osteoporosis and tooth decay. With advancing age high blood levels of calcium lead to calcification of soft tissues causing muscle contractions and stiffness; calcification of endocrine glands, especially the pineal gland and the ovaries; arteriosclerosis, kidney stones, and calcification of the kidneys ultimately leading to kidney failure. Boron deficiency combined with magnesium deficiency is especially damaging to the bones and teeth.

Also other beneficial effects have been reported such as improvement of heart problems, vision, psoriasis, balance, memory and cognition.
>
> As boron is essential for cell membranes and boron deficiency widespread, this may be an important cause for the initiation of tumour growth. Boron compounds have anti-tumour properties and are "potent anti-osteoporotic, anti-inflammatory, hypolipemic, anti-coagulant and anti-neoplastic agents" (1).
>
> patients with rheumatoid arthritis commonly experienced a Herxheimer reaction and that this is always a good prognostic sign. They must persevere and in another 2 or 3 weeks the pain, swelling and stiffness will be gone. (4,5)
>
> The Herxheimer reaction is an early aggravation of symptoms with increased pain. It is commonly due to toxins released by killed Candida and mycoplasma. This is very common with antimicrobial therapy, and borax definitely is an exceptionally good and strong fungicide. What surprises me, however, is that this fungicidal effect is already present at this rather low dose of 75 to 90 mg of borax.
>
> One woman wrote in a recent Nexus Magazine of curing her Lupus and serious kidney disease in 4 month with half a teaspoon of daily borax powder.
>
> Borax, similar to the equally endangered Lugol's iodine solution, can also be used to remove accumulated fluoride and heavy metals from the body (14). Fluoride not only causes bones to deteriorate, but also the pineal gland to calcify and the thyroid to become underactive. Borax reacts with fluoride ions to form boron fluorides which are then excreted in the urine.
>
> [http://www.health-science-spirit.com/borax.htm](http://www.health-science-spirit.com/borax.htm)

> Life Extension Magazine
>
> **Boron**

calcium d-glucarate is a fabulous supplement which basically enables glucoronidation (a phase II liver enzyme process) - weirdly, if you have pathogenic bacteria, they will eat the sugar molecule ("gluco" in glucoronidation) thus killing the process - then, you are left with an oxidized version of whatever toxin got oxidized in phase I liver process - taking calcium d-glucarate inhibits the bugs from metabolizing that sugar molecule - a very basic explanation because I am no biochemist...

In fact, I read somewhere that you could take all 3 of the aforementioned supplements and raise testosterone with them...

The topic is:
**How to create as good as possible artificial light.**

When one creates artificial light, he/she usually will try to recreate some ideal light that does not happen naturally at his/her latitude.

That ideal light condition will be as it is on equator or nearby.

UVA light does not happen at night in any place.
So one conclusion is to not use UVA at night.

OTOH, dr K told us about healthy restaurant crew because it was exposed to UV light for decades.
Also he placed black light in his daughter's bedroom.

So it appears that UVA light may not hurt when used at times other than normal.

I tried using my black light at night during sleep time, it made me uncomfortable, I stopped using it after few nights.

> Nicotine has dramatic effect on cytochrome 1 tunneling of electrons: What is that effect? It lowers the % heteroplasmy in mitochondria. Why? It pulses superoxide while shortening the respiratory complex path to stimulate mitophagy to get rid of shitty mitochondria using programs built into to it. If the mitochondria is really bad..........and nicotine won't work you might consider adding methylene blue and a large dash of sunlight to the RPE. FYI: This is a biohack from something I've written.
> Random thought of the week that should stun you: Half the bestselling drugs in the US target the product of a circadian gene yet pharma ignores chronobiology...........share it with your fellow MD's, family, friends and your patients.
> For example take the best selling drugs, the statins, do they target circadian cycles? Yep. How? They block the Q cycle moving electrons from cytochrome 1 to 3 to increase the distance electrons can tunnel. Every increase in one Angstroms slows tunneling speeds by a factor of ten. As tunneling speeds slow what occurs? Inflammation and swelling. What is inflammation at its fundamental level? It is the excess of protons and/or loss of electrons at your surfaces. The surfaces being the eye, gut, and skin. pH is a function of proton concentration; low pH = high proton concentration = inflammation = lower EZ in water. Water is a chromophore for red light in the solar spectrum. Once the EZ is formed by proteins filled with electrons touching water, the EZ grows massively when 270 nm UV light hits a surface. If you cannot build an EZ what kind of life do you get? Optimal or suboptimal? Do you think a device designed to deliver purple and red light using surface cooling might have an effect on performance since it acts on this fundamental mechanism? I bet many Quantlet beta testers are already writing down bio-hacks as they read this. The loss of an EZ around the mitochondria for any reason increases the distance between respiratory proteins at the atomic level measured by Angstroms too. DO you see how the link of statins and a loss of EZ are similar? Key take home repeatable point: for every increase of 1 Angstrom, the rate of quantum tunneling of electrons drops ten fold. Do you think that kind of redox drop has a small effect or no effect on bio-energenics in a cell? Or might this be exactly how heteroplasmy develops? Is this why statins cause cognitive delay and metabolic syndrome in tissues with high mitochondrial density? Statins are well known to increase the distance electrons must travel via tunneling. Tiny shifts in your thinking, and even smaller changes in your energy status in mitochondria can lead to massive alterations of your results and the diseases you get. This is why people get sick today biophysically. People need to know this data. They also need to know that supplementing ubiquinol is highly beneficial for "statin patients only" because it shortens the distance between the same proteins to lower the energy barrier for quantum tunneling of electrons. Nicotine, MB, and red light all shortens the respiratory chain............because they all do some interesting things to the EZ around the mitochondria called the MINOS. Time 8 has massive implications...............[http://1.bp.blogspot.com/-K\_zs6\_edb...LBeJH\_9g/s1600/mitofamilyinheritanceimage.gif](http://1.bp.blogspot.com/-K_zs6_edbzc/UWxPueFWy3I/AAAAAAAAAdY/mcPLBeJH_9g/s1600/mitofamilyinheritanceimage.gif)

Yeah, that's how I do it - works really well - in fact, not to piss you off or anything, I have been using it to get rid of all those hallides with zero side effects... using, EE12, I take acetyl-l-cysteine+glutamine+glycine when I get up after drowning in Lugol's (hemeroid was mostly gone after the first application BTW and it's only 2%), this I chase with an alpha lipoic acid (because not sure if the disulphide bonds in the glutathione reductase are live because of dehydration - I think that was either Time 8 or EE 12 -this must be why Andy Cutler thinks glutathione won't work for mercury because we are all so dehydrated) then I chase that with a BH2 and tyrosine and of course selenium... and a b vitamin - I put Lugol's on my thyroid, breasts, vagina, rectum and zero side effects - I can't believe I just said this on the internet... get over yourself Pen... I was getting an eye twitch but with this protocol nada - then I go outside butte naked with no glasses and ground for at least an hour - I've also been putting acetyl-l-carnosine drops in my eye - for the glycation - plus it's wet and melanin splits water - so hey, ya need to be wet - since I have a massive trampoline, I get on that baby for a few minutes because it clears the lymph and gets circulation moving - this brings the iron in the blood vessels close to the surface for the ol' photoelectric effect - plus the sweat adds water on the skin for the same -

I have also added mag citrate powder to the glutamine and acetyl-l-carnitine powder also - I think that was in the knolls and/or cortisol blog post - CPC 4/EE12/Time 8 have been blowing my mind - oh yeah, I make sure and add zinc with carnosine at some point because it is required for glutathione reductase - and I believe I am storing iron - and many other metals - due to low zinc... yeah I eat oysters but not raw - one more thing I do is in the shower, I rub Epsom salts all over so the photo effect will work better - I think that was Tensegrity 7 -

after that I eat breakfast which is a lot of greens (think nitrogen to slow down the vibrations of blue light plus cilantro chelates metals) and DHA eggs - and sometimes smoked salmon -

The plan is to install an outdoor shower, which I think will work better but sweating on a tramp also gives the skin water for melanin to split... oh yeah, I add a dropper of MB into the powdered water to get the redox up and then go into the sun - with this little morning routine, I have a steady energy all day - the thinking is clearer also - I might take another hit of MB in the afternoon or at lunch, but not usually - I have also had good luck with running a UVA light over my bed after the shower at night when my skin is kinda damp - last night, I actually felt a rush of warmth and I was lying there with nothing on and no sheets on top of me - very comfortable -

My immediate concern is iron - high ferritin - which if I'm fathoming any of this correctly, means glutathione is low -and I'm storing transition metals - my best guess is because of lack of zinc - not sure why zinc would be low for one with high blood sugar unless you pee that out too Along with magnesium - oh yeah, I also spray mag oil on the back of my legs after the morning shower for the above reason - I actually have no idea how I ended up in this blather fest but there ya go

Why I feel extra stupid is I never put together that high ferritin would be a party for biofilms for bacteria... this is why my immediate goal is to lower it... hopefully, upping the redox will do that - if not, if anyone else has any ideas how to lower it, I'm all ears This is probably why when one drinks apple cider vinegar - actually any vinegar - it would break down the biofilms on the gut wall and thin it out - as per Time 8 - this would let more light in or through (not quite sure how that works on the GALT yet) - but glutathione should be live if you do that kind of thing because it seems to me that's a lot of detox going on - especially since the darlings love using metals in their biofilms -

>

if they are high
slow down on consuming food containing iron.

### Penny — 2016-03-05T04:24:51+0200

This is what I was going for... :
Zinc Deficiency-induced Iron Accumulation, a Consequence
of Alterations in Iron Regulatory Protein-binding Activity,
Iron Transporters, and Iron Storage Proteins\*
Received for publication, November 5, 2007, and in revised form, December 4, 2007 Published, JBC Papers in Press, December 11, 2007, DOI 10.1074/jbc.M709043200
Brad J. Niles‡,Michael S. Clegg‡, Lynn A. Hanna‡, Susan S. Chou‡, Tony Y. Momma‡, Heeok Hong§,
and Carl L. Keen‡¶1
From the Departments of ‡Nutrition and ¶Internal Medicine, University of California, Davis, California 95616-8669
and the §Department of Food Service Management and Nutrition, Sangmyung University, Seoul 110–743, Korea
One consequence of zinc deficiency is an elevation in cell and
tissue iron concentrations. To examine the mechanism(s)
underlying this phenomenon, Swiss 3T3 cells were cultured in
zinc-deficient (D, 0.5 M zinc), zinc-supplemented (S, 50 M
zinc), or control (C, 4M zinc) media. After 24 h of culture, cells
in theDgroup were characterized by a 50% decrease in intracellular
zinc and a 35% increase in intracellular iron relative to cells
in the S and C groups. The increase in cellular iron was associated
with increased transferrin receptor 1 protein and mRNA
levels and increased ferritin light chain expression. The divalent
metal transporter 1()iron-responsive element isoform mRNA
was decreased during zinc deficiency-induced iron accumulation.
Examination of zinc-deficient cells revealed increased
binding of iron regulatory protein 2 (IRP2) and decreased binding
of IRP1 to a consensus iron-responsive element. The
increased IRP2-binding activity in zinc-deficient cells coincided
with an increased level of IRP2 protein. The accumulation of
IRP2 protein was independent of zinc deficiency-induced intracellular
nitric oxide production but was attenuated by the addition
of the antioxidant N-acetylcysteine or ascorbate to the D
medium. These data support the concept that zinc deficiency
can result in alterations in iron transporter, storage, and regulatory
proteins, which facilitate iron accumulation.

### JanSz — 2016-03-05T16:46:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-190231>

Penny,
I am reading your posts for a while.
For whatever reason dr K is letting you and few others slide as far as your moniker goes.
It would be good idea to know if you are bunny or human.
if you are human what sex are you?

Anyhow, you do your best, learning vise, but prepare your self for action.
As far as phlebotomy goes.
1\. find out what your labotarory is able to test easily and immediately on the spot, just walking in.
In my case it was a choice between hemoglobin an hematocrit.
In my place they are using 30 seconds hemoglobin test. But only once a week.

In my case, my first choice was blood donation, that could be done per my choice any time.
Turn out, after I (always truthfully) filled up a form, they refused to take my blood.
(There are numerous reasons why they do not want to take somebody's blood,
and basic reason is that it must qualify to be used in transfusing it to someone else who need it.

So my first trip have not ended in phlebotomy.
I used that opportunity to ask what they need from me to do therapeutic phlebotomy.

We worked out a script like this:
(Time passed by, you have to translate
ICD-9 289.0 Secondary Polycythemia
to
ICD-10 XYZW Secondary Polycythemia

Script for therapeutic phlebotomy

John DOE DOB-day/month/year
elevated Hgb levels
check once a month,
perform therapeutic phlebotomy
if Hgb>=15.8
repeat 12 months
289.0 Secondary Polycythemia
\----
\---------
Note the cutoff 15.8 (I decided on that number by my self)
first --I am a man
my top laboratory range for hemoglobin is 18
wise people think that ideal Hgb=~16
other reasons, facing raising Hgb it is good to work out flexibility
since at first you do not know how far Hgb will fall, and how quick Hgb will be raising after phlebotomy
latter when you know
how quick your blood is restoring high Hgb
how often you must check your Hgb in practice
how accessible is your personal schedule
how quickly you can get phlebotomy
you will work out your personal schedule

in practice, with script like that,
that is valid for one year,
at first you can check your Hgb not more that once a month
(for severe cases, month my be too long waiting)
you do not have to give out blood if you find your Hgb>15.8
you make your own decision on the spot
(in my case I would wait till I see about Hgb~17-17.5
some wait till they see 18,
daring people wait even longer

In any case, I suggest that you do not want to be too daring.

All the best.

...
Say,
these days, I imagine that in Greece things are little bit tense, and not all services are available immediately. So make your best intelligent bets.

...
If you are more daring than average, (or just desperate),
specially when you have helping friend near.
there is enough hardware available on amazon, that some may want to try do your own.
That reminds me of a guy in Texas plus his lovely DW.
With current health care (Obama care), very low income, and love of motorcycles, things happen.

..
Leaches are also available, have not heard much about them lately.

..

Nope:
Light builds hormones by building dopamine using aromatic amino acids that absorb UV light. It does this to cortisol too. That light is used to power up electrons and this electrons have to get assigned a spin in mitochondria. So light acts as the currency in the compound pharmacy in our pituitary every day to make things we need from light. If you understand factorial math, that means within our octave of the visible spectrum that retinal cells that control bio chemicals can handle 8,683,317,618,811,886,495,518,194,401,280,000,000 different frequencies. This is a staggering level of power and control. So when you open up any biochemistry book and realize that biochemistry only uses 100,000 substrate in reactions you realize light can control it. When you factor in that the photoelectric effect acts instantaneously, with no time delay, then you begin to see how 100,000 biochemical reactions can occur per second using light frequencies from the visible spectrum easily.

People tell us all the time to block the sun with sunscreens and glass. Nonsense. We have built in natural sunscreens for UV light. Vitamin C is one way in how we modulate light assimilation and transport at surfaces.....This is why equatorial fruits have vitamin c in them. It is also why taking too much vitamin C is bad.........it block the sun even more. What else can happens to SHBG because of an altered copper metabolism linked to Vitamin C? When there is a mismatch in the Vitamin C signaling in collagen and it is not optimized you can see your SHBG in the plasma rise and many problems then begin in your sex steroid hormone cascades crash and burn..........WHY LACK of sunlight. Dopamine is the guardian of the gland. ...........This is why SHBG is so misunderstood by your steroid guru's and anti- aging docs. When it rises your body has no light contained in its system and % heteroplasmy is rising.............What to do? MOVE to the sun because modern life is lowering it yield every day around you.

A rising SHBG is a beacon of a light problem for the person with the condition of poor light assimilation.............It affects ceruloplasmin and the carotenoids cycles in the skin and without dopamine in the eye, you make no hormones and the ones you do make are released incorrectly.
DNA breakdown on the surface layers of the skin is the another ingenious way to do it using non linear optics.........and then their is the carotenoid system on surfaces as another fail safe for UV light assimilation. This is why vegetarians do OK around the equator and no where else...........veggies have them. You dont need veggies outside the tropics. It worsens UV assimilation. Terry Wahls fail right there folks. This is how a half truth gets you killed or sicker. This redness of the skin is the signal from the sun you need to pay attention too because it means you have reached your dose of UV for the day. If you eat too many veggies or too much fruit outside the tropics it is akin to using chemical sunscreens, so that, you also lose that signal increasing the chance you will burn and fry the system. It just does not make any quantum sense from this perspective to use sunscreen when you have these systems in you. . Just more proof of what people are selling may not be worth buying. Nonsense.

Now Boron is a hack...........but not in the way any of you could imagine............maybe after April 2016 webinar but I doubt it.

### NeilBB — 2016-03-09T21:21:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-190549>

Boron has 3 valence electrons (electron acceptor) and can therefore be used as an agent for semiconductor doping to create "holes." Not only in silicon, but also in carbon nanotubes and graphene. Boron-doped carbon nanotubes have been shown to generate redox advantages including enhanced O2-chemisorbtion and more effective utilization of pi-electrons, which may be very beneficial in biological semiconduction.

Perhaps the quilt reveals the answer? Radiation hormesis? I know recent cancer therapy using boron and x-rays.

The boron tends to go directly to cancer cells. A patient receives an injection of boron that deposits only in the cancer cells.

The patient's body is then bombarded with neutrons that pass through without harming health cells. They then collide with boron atoms. This causes boron to be converted into [lithium](https://forum.jackkruse.com/knowledge/Lithium.html) atoms, alpha particles, and gamma rays. An alpha particle is a helium atom without electrons. Gamma radiation is very high-energy radiation that can kill cells.

The lithium atoms and alpha particles travel only a short distance. They do not leave the cancer cell but have enough energy to kill the cell. Since they do not leave the cell, they pose no threat to healthy cells nearby.

An example of one of the more popular modifications is the implanting of a rare-earth magnet under the skin of one or more fingers. A neodymium magnet is often used; made from an alloy of neodymium, iron and boron, it is a lot stronger than a typical ferrous magnet like one you would find on a fridge. Under optimized conditions, that is, on 15-min exposure of the 20-h grown culture to PEF of the 2,000 V and 20-μs pulse width, accumulation of magnesium in the yeast biomass reached maximum 3.98 mg/g dm. The intermittent supplementation of the cultures with magnesium could even reduce the accumulation efficiency by 30%.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-13T16:42:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-190751>

Magnesium is present in seawater in amounts of about 1300 ppm. After sodium, it is the most commonly found cation in oceans. Rivers contains approximately 4 ppm of magnesium, marine algae 6000-20,000 ppm, and oysters 1200 ppm.
Just drinking water contains between 1 and 5 mg of magnesium per liter.
Magnesium and other alkali earth metals are responsible for water hardness. Water containing large amounts of alkali earth ions is called hard water, and water containing low amounts of these ions is called soft water.

In what way and in what form does magnesium react with water?

Magnesium metals are not affected by water at room temperature. Magnesium generally is a slow-reacting element, but reactivity increases with oxygen levels. Furthermore, magnesium reacts with water vapor to magnesium hydroxide and hydrogen gas:

Mg (s) + 2H2O(g) -> Mg(OH)2(aq) + H2(g)

Magnesium fires cannot be extinguished by water! Magnesium continues to burn even after oxygen is depleted.

Now why do 56 biochemical processes use Mg to generate energy in a cell? Anyone?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-13T16:43:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-190752>

Now look at a periodic table............what do you notice?

[](https://www.facebook.com/jack.kruse)
Magnesium is a chemical element with symbol Mg and atomic number 12. (Dec 2016 webinar alert) It is a shiny gray solid which bears a close physical resemblance to the other five elements in the second column (Group 2, or alkaline earth metals) of the periodic table: they each have the same electron configuration in their outer electron shell producing a similar crystal structure.

Magnesium is the ninth most abundant element in the universe. WOW that is a fact huh? Important or a waste of time? It is synthesized in large, aging stars from the sequential addition of three helium nuclei to a carbon nucleus. When such a star explodes as a supernova, much of its magnesium is expelled into the interstellar medium, where it can be recycled into new star systems. Consequently, magnesium is the eighth most abundant element in the Earth's crust and the fourth most common element in the Earth (below iron, oxygen and silicon), making up 13% of the planet's mass and a large fraction of the planet's mantle. It is the third most abundant element dissolved in seawater, after sodium and chlorine. Funny........oysters and CSF use those too.........hum?

Magnesium only occurs naturally in combination with other elements, where it invariably has a +2 oxidation state. The free element (metal) can be produced artificially, and is highly reactive (though once produced, it is coated in a thin layer of oxide, which partly inhibits this reactivity). The free metal burns with a characteristic brilliant-white light, making it a useful ingredient in flares. The metal is obtained by "electrolysis of magnesium salts obtained from brine". Brine is salt water huh?

Magnesium is the eleventh most abundant element by mass in the human body. WOW.......that is interesting fact huh? Important or not? Its ions are essential to all cells in most animals including humans. The enzymes that need it are usually mitochondrial based? HUH? They interact with polyphosphate compounds such as ATP, DNA, and RNA which all use proton tunneling with protons. Protons = H+ = the element found in the matrix.........interesting huh? Hundreds of enzymes require magnesium ions to function. Magnesium compounds are used medicinally as common laxatives, antacids (e.g., milk of magnesia), and to stabilize abnormal nerve excitation or blood vessel spasm such as in eclampsia. Magnesium ions are sour to the taste, and in low concentrations they help impart a natural tartness to fresh mineral waters. Magnesium is the metallic ion at the center of chlorophyll surrounded by 4 Nitrogens to interact with sunlight to drive photosynthesis and forms the exciton of chlorophyl.......hey doesn't photosynthesis form the basis of the entire food web? Interesting huh? Mg2+ is a common additive to fertilizers so plants need it for Rubisco......................Does Rubisco have a Mg ion in the center surrounded by 4 Nitrogens = photosynthesis = the basis of the entire food web?

> Magnesium is present in seawater in amounts of about 1300 ppm. After sodium, it is the most commonly found cation in oceans. Rivers contains approximately 4 ppm of magnesium, marine algae 6000-20,000 ppm, and oysters 1200 ppm.
> Just drinking water contains between 1 and 5 mg of magnesium per liter.
> Magnesium and other alkali earth metals are responsible for water hardness. Water containing large amounts of alkali earth ions is called hard water, and water containing low amounts of these ions is called soft water.
>
> In what way and in what form does magnesium react with water?
>
> Magnesium metals are not affected by water at room temperature. Magnesium generally is a slow-reacting element, but reactivity increases with oxygen levels. Furthermore, magnesium reacts with water vapor to magnesium hydroxide and hydrogen gas:
>
> Mg (s) + 2H2O(g) -> Mg(OH)2(aq) + H2(g)
>
> Magnesium fires cannot be extinguished by water! Magnesium continues to burn even after oxygen is depleted.
>
> Now why do 56 biochemical processes use Mg to generate energy in a cell? Anyone?

This was interesting..[http://cosmology.com/QuantumConsciousness108.html](http://cosmology.com/QuantumConsciousness108.html) found it as I was searching Brownian motion. The whole article is rich with rabbit holes and cites but this stuck out:

**The issue of quantum processes in the brain has received a lot of interest. Neuronal decoherence processes have only been calculated while assuming that ions, such as K+, are believed to undergo quantum Brownian motion (e.g. Tegmark, 2000). Extremely short decoherence timescales of 10-20 seconds, result. Such arguments though assume that the system in question is in thermal equilibrium with its environment, which is not typically the case for bio-molecular dynamics (e.g. Frohlich, 1986; Mesquita et. al., 2005; Porkony and Wu, 1998). Also, the ions themselves do not move freely within the ion-channel filter, but rather their states are pre-selected, leading to possible protection of quantum coherence within the ion channel for a time scale on the order of 10-3 seconds at 300K, ~ time scale of ion-channel opening and closing (e.g. Bernroider and Roy, 2005). _Entangled states of K+ ions and oxygen atoms within the channels are likely occurring._**

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-190971>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=187584)
>
> In the last century, new information about the effects of methylene blue has accumulated, making it possible to reinterpret former work on the subject, and the research directly guided by Ehrlich's ideas, including the discovery of the sulfa drugs, anti-inflammatory and antipsychotic drugs.
>
> Ideas of cellular coherence, polariz-ability of molecules and cells, liquid crystalline phase changes, and other non-local properties of molecular systems have established a basis for a complete revision of the "cell model" that dominated the 20th century.
>
> The lock-and-key idea, as applied to "receptors," enzymes, and antibodies, implies that a closely complementary spatial arrangement of specific atoms determines the biological effect. Several types of evidence show that there is more to cellular activation than a good spatial and chemical fit between effector and receptor. For example, Luca Turin (e.g., Franco, et al., 2011) has shown (in people and animals) that the way molecules vibrate governs the way they smell. Molecules with different shapes, but similar vibrations, smell the same, molecules with similar shapes, but different vibrations, don't smell the same. When deuterium (hydrogen that contains a neutron as well as a proton in its nucleus) is substituted in a molecule for hydrogen, the molecule has the same shape, but vibrates differently, and fruit flies can tell the difference by smell. They will reject a favored food when its molecules contain the heavy form of hydrogen. In Turin's view, such effects are transmitteed through proteins by alterations in the state of their electrons.
>
> Methylene blue is a potent cationic dye with **maximum absorption of light around 670 nm.** The specifics of absorption depend on a number of factors, including protonation, adsorption to other materials, and metachromasy - the formation of dimers and higher-order aggregates depending on concentration and other interactions:
>
> Species / Absorption peak / Extinction coefficient (dm3/mole·cm)
> MB+ (soln) 664 95000
> MBH2+ (soln) 741 76000
> (MB+)2 (soln) 605 132000
> (MB+)3 (soln) 580 110000
>
> Solutions of this substance are blue when in an oxidizing environment, but will turn colorless if exposed to a reducing agent. The redox properties can be seen in a classical demonstration of chemical kinetics in general chemistry, the "blue bottle" experiment. Typically, a solution is made of glucose (dextrose), methylene blue, and sodium hydroxide. Upon shaking the bottle, oxygen oxidizes methylene blue, and the solution turns blue. The dextrose will gradually reduce the methylene blue to its colorless, reduced form. Hence, when the dissolved dextrose is entirely consumed, the solution will turn blue again.
> **In this sense, the home hack can be as simple as applying methylene blue solution (say 400mcg dissolved in a 20%ethanol solution, perhaps with \*benzoic acid - a 1% solution) to the skin to test local tissue hypoxia. if the stain of the MB disappears within only a number of hours (say 4-8) you could say that the local tissue is hypoxic)** **This points to the property that MB can act as a substitute terminal electron acceptor in place of O2. This does not require the help of a doctor, as a lifetime supply of MB to be used topically can be purcahsed for about $8-21 at an aquarium store. Those withing to take it orally may want to cource pharmaceutical grade (i do not know where the differences lie, likely in purity)**
> _\*benzoic acid had a number of cool properties. First, it uncouples mitochondria in extremely low concentrations (less than 1picoM) and that uncoupling capacity does NOT increase much with dosage. So, it has a very wide range of effectiveness and safety unlike drugs such as DNP which can kill you if you miscalculate the dosage just a bit. Benzoc acid also reduces ammonia in the body and stimulates the urea cycle. This property is helpful since many people suffer from ammonia overload cause by slow metabolism and stress. Finally, benzoic acid is very well absorbed topically and orally and is not known to affect negatively organs like the liver or the digestive system._
> ****topical use of MB: [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7708136](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/7708136)**
> **
> So, Methylene blue can form a link between a cell's reductants, such as cysteine and glutathione, and its oxidants, including oxygen and hydrogen peroxide. By oxidizing cysteines (Crowe, et al., 2013), it can prevent the polymerization of microtubules.
> Microtubules, which make up the mitotic apparatus that separates the chromosomes and divides the cytoplasm when cells are proliferating, activate the enzyme that synthesizes nitric oxide. Nitric oxide blocks oxidative energy production, and activates the characteristic cancer metabolism, aerobic glycolysis (Brix, et al., 2012), creating the state of "pseudohypoxia."
>
> When an inflammation was created experimentally to test the ability of blood cells to transmigrate through endothelial cells, treatment with methylene blue increased the transendothelial movement of granulocytes, T- and B-lymphocytes, and monocytes, but it didn't have that effect on a T-cell line derived from a person with leukemia (Werner, et al., 2013), showing that it was a selective process, rather than just an increased permeability. An excess of nitric oxide increases general permeability of blood vessels.
>
> Methylene blue, by its ability to be reduced and oxidized, can restore respiration to cells whose mitochondria have been damaged in various ways, even by cyanide (Barron, 1930). That's involved in its recognized use to treat shock, correcting the inability of mitochondria to use oxygen. Nitric oxide blocks the use of oxygen in shock, and methylene blue blocks the synthesis of nitric oxide, while at the same time it activates mitochondrial energy production. Besides the restoration of cellular respiration, methylene blue probably acts more directly on cellular structure and sensitivity, for example by oxidizing sulfhydryl groups that regulate the "excitatory amino acid receptor," providing protection against excitotoxity, again functioning in direct opposition to nitric oxide.
>
> Methylene blue is being investigated as a treatment for the major brain degenerative diseases, psychoses, anxiety, depression, and cancer. In a small daily dose of 15 milligrams, in a well controlled study, it was effective against severe depression (Naylor, et al., 1987), and many animal studies show that it is effective against anxiety. There are probably many things involved in these effects on the nervous system--the anti-excitatory action, a variety of anti-estrogen effects, and increase of thyroid hormone while decreasing pituitary hormones--but they all seem to involve protection against nitric oxide.
>
> Over-production of nitric oxide is involved in many of the symptoms of viral infections, including herpes, and the therapeutic effects of methylene blue suggests that natural resistance to viral infections might depend on maintaining a safely oxidizing redox balance.
>
> Reversal of cancer metabolism by methylene blue (Poteet, et al., 2013) is another of its effects that probably involves reversal of the effects of nitric oxide, since nitric oxide promotes aerobic glycolysis and tumor growth (Caneba, et al., 2014).
> **SUMMARY:
>
> Methylene blue has the properties of DISASSOCIATING NO from Cytochrome C Oxidase in a similar way to red light (no surprise given its absorption spectrum) as well as inhibiting the synthesis of new NO molecules, AND taking the place of oxygen as a terminal electron acceptor, essentially transporting electrons directly from complex 1 to CCO and in the process creating no ROS. this is essentially clean efficient electron transfer with no ill effects at correct dosage.**
> Again something like 400-800mcg in solution as mentioned above taken orally should show no negative effects, but topical absorption (whilst leaving a stain) has been shown to have excellent properties.
>
> If people are worried about serotonin syndrome taking MB with caffiene should mitigate any effects due to its dopaminergic properties. or you can take microdose LSD if you are so inclined
>
> Other:
> [http://selfhacked.com/2013/08/25/methylene-blue-the-cheapest-cognitive-enhancer/](http://selfhacked.com/2013/08/25/methylene-blue-the-cheapest-cognitive-enhancer/)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-05-02T01:08:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-194001>

Neil is right about women. Throughout evolution male tend to become what females wanted them to be. Most people don't know history well. Food guru's all point to natural selection from Darwin's 1859 thesis but they all forget that he shifted focus in 1871 to sexual selection. Why? He cut his teeth on the idea that an organism survived if it could navigate the rigors of its environment but it still would be wholly unsuccessful if it could also not be successful sexually to pass its genome on. This is why human men are way different than other primates from an evolutionary standpoint. We remain brutal with our animus but we tone it down with our anima.........our ape male counter parts are brutes in this department. Big brains have a lot to do with that adaptation.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-05-02T01:14:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/2015-heteroplasmy-bio-hack-results-nicotine-and-mb.17445/post-194002>

Both natural selection (gene survival) and sexual selection ( reproduction upside) are a give and take non linear process. They both deal with ongoing simultaneous feedback loops that are coupled between the local environment, individuals, and culture that are the driving force compelling our species to alter their shape, size, behavior, and metabolism. This relationship between mitochondria and nuclear genome is continuous, with a chronic ebb and flow that creates a fluid dynamic within our cell's water that a linear biologic narrative will never adequately convey..........a quantum one, that is non linear and optically based has zero such constraints.

# Thread: Antidepressants can make you WORSE!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antidepressants-can-make-you-worse.17449/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Cpt.Tired — 2016-01-30T07:44:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antidepressants-can-make-you-worse.17449/post-187660>

I just want to give a heads up to anybody who is currently taking an antidepressant that might not know this fact.
The 'therapeutic window' is different for everyone so if you are feeling shitty on an antidepressant or maybe even feeling WORSE, it is possible that the dose is too high for you..even at a dose that is considered 'low' by your doctor.

This has happened to me twice now.

A long time ago my doctor (back then-1991) wrote me a prescription for me to titrate up to 300mg of Fluvoxamine. When I got up to 100mg I felt much WORSE! I dropped it down to 75mg and that worked.
My Dr. was very surprised that I got benefit from such a low dose stating that I was "the only person that he had ever seen get benefit from such a low dose".
This could be one of the reasons that there is a suicide link to antidepressants. The therapeutic window can vary hugely from person to person and I honestly do not think that there is enough awareness of this fact.

Hopefully this little bit of info will help someone who is struggling out there....
Better yet-meditate, sunlight on your RPE, deep breathing, DHA, CT, epi-paleo gives much more than an antidepressant will ever give...
..freedom from Big Pharma is such an awesome feeling!

### BrainWhisperer — 2016-02-08T05:39:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/antidepressants-can-make-you-worse.17449/post-188509>

Especially for the younger cohort...

[http://www.bmj.com/content/352/bmj.i545](http://www.bmj.com/content/352/bmj.i545)

# Thread: Newbie questions, Please Help!!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-questions-please-help.17451/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 25
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Allanah — 2016-01-31T07:31:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-questions-please-help.17451/post-187742>

Hi everyone! I am new as far as being a member so bear with me.
I have tons of new questions, but I'll start with a little background. I am a 15 year old girl, have been a little overweight since I was put on steroids when young, I live in South Dakota, and I think that's about all that's relevant right now. I am part of a family of 8 so it's pretty tight with money so things like fresh seafood are hard to come by, but I'm making it work with canned fish and some frozen, plus a lot of our own skeletal meat. I have started the leptin rx January 1 and that has been going well, but I have a lot of questions.
First off, I assumed my hormones would react the same as an adults, but the Leptin Rx should still work for me, right? Everything so far has been exactly as Jack described so it makes sense, but I thought I'd ask.
Also, when the signs of LS start showing up and I start HIIT and heavy weights, is the INSANITY program a good HIIT program? Any other suggestions?
Now for some random questions that have been coming up between my brother (age 16) and I:
What is the opinion on pork rinds? Obviously inferior to seafood, but as far as once in a while or while on the go, are they optimal or not? And why?
Until I can find another source of oysters, my only option is canned, smoked oysters in cottonseed oil. Do the benefits of the oysters outweigh the bad things about the oil, or should I avoid them since I have salmon, shrimp, tilapia, mackerel, clams, etc. daily?
I have been a bit overweight as long as I can remember, but other than that I am healthy, though I am allergic to dairy (which isn't a problem since I've been eating Paleo or Epi-Paleo for over a year), so what approach do I take to everything? As a sick person or healthy? For example would I use nightshades or not? Or when it says "Healthy people may use more carbs" does that include me or do I stick to the "sick" recommendations?

And my most important question for now, what things can I do right now that would make me closer to optimal? I have many limitations with my environment and financially, so certain things are out until I make enough money on my own, but what realistic things can I do to help my health the most? (I already drink 2 gallons of RO water daily, so that's a bonus!)

Thank you guys so much for your time, I can't wait until I actually understand all this and can help more people like me. Right now I have limited understanding so you may have to dumb it down some but I'll do my best to understand everything. Thanks again!

### lilreddgirl — 2016-01-31T23:33:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-questions-please-help.17451/post-187786>

Welcome

I think it will still work for you, it seems to have worked for some boys your age on forum so maybe same for girls:

The latest stuff that's touted as very important is Light:

looking at sunrise every morning while blinking for at least 10 min but can go on for hours...

ideally exposing skin to it too...

and also be outside and look at morning light after sunrise up until 10 or 11 AM while blinking to get morning UV rays that come on about 30 min after sunrise.... and beneficial to expose as much skin as possible too. As much time as you can spend outside in the morning is best.

Also wearing blueblocker glasses at night. You do not want blue light in your eye after sunset. Use an amber or red light bulb for after sunset light.

You can also supplement during the day with indoor UV lights like Solar Glo 160 bulb (requires a lamp that can handle the high watts so you'd get one of those too, like one that can handle 250watt bulb).

You can also use blacklight bulbs to supplement and these are ok even in evening light... Duracell brand works or a Led (I didn't like Feit brand).

You can also get an IR bulb and use it to do therapy sessions for health and energy.

\---
Also you can CT protocol and that makes a big difference for a lot of people - it's in the blog - start with face dunks, don't skip them... (also CT will eventually get you used to being able to expose skin to sun while in the cold).

\----
Make sure you are not sleeping or spending lots of time near EMF. Such as a Smart Meter or Wifi. Don't sleep with your phone in bedroom. EMF environment is VERY impactful for health.

\_\_\_\_

I would use nightshades unless you notice they bother you - some people do ok with them. Generally do not use more carbs though especially through the winter in a low UV cold environment like South Dakota. You can eat onions and mushrooms year round though, and also greens and well I eat asparagus and herbs and seasonings. The idea is it could be beneficial to be ketogenic in winter...

When spring comes in March THEN add in more carbs and don't be ketogenic through summer.

Pork rinds should be ok, but as far as on the go remember 'no snacking.' Have 2 (or 3, or even 1) full meals a day.

I would not have cottonseed oil.

I'm not sure if Insanity is good as HIIT someone else might comment on that, make another thread and ask just about that? It can be difficult to get answers on forum for me sometimes... I would start light, like doing a few sets of 40 yard running sprints... this is actually something I'm working on putting into my own practice.

>

Sea Veggies are always in season. I order them online in bulk for savings, prices vary, but this company I use [http://www.herbco.com/](http://www.herbco.com/) is on the low side... 4$ a pound for kelp for example while sometimes seaweed in stores might be up to 6$ an ounce! You can use the search function to find what seaweeds you would like as they have so many other herbs too.

I have experience with apple cider vinegar. I table spoon in 1/2 cup of water 10-30 minutes before a meal 3 times a day works very well for midsection weightloss for some people. It also gives an energy boost. Dr. K mentioned vinegar briefly as something that can give a beneficial superoxide boost. Type of ACV used I believe matters quite a bit for how healthy it might be, Bragg's is a great brand or you can try another - you want something that is organic and/or unfiltered.

### Scompy — 2016-02-02T02:44:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/newbie-questions-please-help.17451/post-187904>

Welcome!

I usually get my vinegar from pickles these days.. I used to do ACV several years ago, but felt my teeth way more sensitive doing it. I'm thinking apple cider vinegar can slowly remove the enamel/calcium from the teeth over time--If you do it, it's probably good to use a straw and rinse after drinking to minimize getting ACV on the teeth. There has been claims about cardiovascular benefits of keeping arteries/veins/heart valves from being calcified, or even reversing calcification. I take those claims with a grain of salt...it's a far more complex issue in why the body wants to calcify the cardiovascular system in the first place. Look at Type2 Diabetics. They often develop calcification of the arterties after many years. This is not widely talked about, and it's also why my mother passed away 2 years ago. A higher-carb diet with insulin injections is a recipe for calcification over time from my perspective. But you get faster calcification when calcium efflux is a norm because of a higher nnEMF environment. Then for the Type2 Diabetic, you've gotta ask how many get their morning sunlight into their eyes and onto their skin?

### colt — 2016-02-04T14:33:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hiit-questions.17454/post-188171>

Tobia
Here is a thought. Pull the bar down (or initiate the thought of pulling- Lats and biceps) in the bench press. Most just lower the bar. Pecs elongate on the way down while lats and biceps pull the bar down the way we do it. The the motion becomes ALL muscles invloved- doing their job. Successive Induction

# Thread: Too much omega 3?

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RyanMSauce — 2016-02-01T08:47:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-omega-3.17457/post-187835>

I just started eating a lot of salmon roe and I'm wondering if there is such thing as too much omega 3? I get at least 5 grams a day now. Sometimes maybe 10 I think. I probably am eating more omega 3 than omega 6.

From what I read too much plain fish oil is not good because it is prone to oxidation but maybe coming from a natural unprocessed source that doesn't happen?

Im doing good. I am getting probably 2 hours of direct sunlight and 4 hours in and out of the sun in my Arizona room with doors and windows open. So even if I don't get direct sunlight im still getting the natural sunlight in the shade.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-02-01T14:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/too-much-omega-3.17457/post-187848>

There is too much for anything..............but using a supplement is not wise for DHA

### RyanMSauce — 2016-02-01T20:09:35+0200
