[https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/you-...-jack-kruse?trk=hp-feed-article-title-publish](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/you-entangled-your-kid-via-morning-sickness-jack-kruse?trk=hp-feed-article-title-publish)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-17T19:15:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/optimal-kids-begin-without-morning-sickness.17693/post-191088>

When pregnant make sure you eat within that 30 minute window of the Leptin Rx...........why?

We are designed to eat in the sun light.......why? Every electron has 4 quantum numbers and then has spin. Those numbers and spin get reassigned in mitochondria during the day. That is why mitochondria are circadian quantum nano electromagnets. The quantum number for electrons are n, l, M1, m2. “n” gives the total energy of the electron, “l” gives its angular momentum, and m1 determines the angle between angular momentum of the electron and an external magnetic field. This gives us the extent of the magnetic contribution of the total energy of the entire atom. All 3 of these numbers are quantized. M2 is the fourth number and this is about electron spin. Electron spin links to the Pauli exclusion principle forbidding any two electrons having the same quantum state. In other words, they must be different. Processing of your food electrons must be done during the day to make sure there is non uniformity of your electrons from food.

**Subforum:** The Cave

### jackware — 2016-03-19T06:05:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/made-my-own-diy-quantlet.17697/post-191178>

During my 90 minute commute to work between 1pm and 2:30 pm each day I rest my left hand on the window sill, wrist facing up. The sun hits those veins and the wind cools my hand and arm real quick. After about 5-10 minutes I bring it inside the truck to warm back up and then repeat the process. Not bad, huh?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-191199>

So I'm just sitting around on the balcony, cloudy day, and it just came to my mind that I have always been "sensitive" to light, meaning that I have to squint my eyes as soon as I look upwards a little bit. Is this an indication for something? I have myopia and wear contacts and I want to try and see if I might be able to get through the day without them (-2,25, not driving a car but cycling to work). I think it's only sunlight making me squint and no indoor-lights, not even my recently purchased osram vitalux bulbs which have uv-a and uv-b.

You lucky guy. I have -6,0 on both eyes. But same here, crying in the sun

### Inger — 2016-03-24T09:54:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-191427>

### Butters — 2016-03-28T14:01:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-191641>

He remember hearing Jack in the podcast saying his wife in the beginning had to cry too. But finally it stopped. So I just keep on jamming DHA and sungazing. Its seems I am not as sensitive anymore as my girlfriends and mother. But in bright light I cry too, but not as much as in the beginning.
But lately I can feel deep joy when looking at the sun and standing barefoot at the ground. Its like meditating and relaxing.

Look what most animals do. Cats and dogs lying in the sun always look at the sun. And these animals get there portions of DHA with the food.

### endless — 2016-04-06T04:10:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-192147>

My eyes have always been very sensitive to bright light. Plus I've always sneezed in the sun. Lately my tolerance has increased greatly and now I am focusing on NOT squinting when in the sun. I think I squint out of habit sometimes, so when I notice it I consciously relax my face. When I do that I notice that I don't really need to squint.

### Butters — 2016-04-19T16:31:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193023>

I now have a short daily adaptation period, in which I have to blink more but it disappears fast. We will see how stronger sunlight act on me than UVI 5

### seanb4 — 2016-04-19T20:48:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193037>

About a month ago I was having trouble laying down with my eyes CLOSED in the direction of the sun at mid day (UV 2 probably) without them watering massively and wanting to turn my head.

I've just come out the garden after 6hrs in the sun and about 1-2 total time actually looking right at at it or just to the side with no real watering, lots of blinking and I have to look away every minute or so but still, massive progress.

As for my earlier post I have a theory or 2 on why solar glo would causes eye burn. I think it has much higher UV than other frequencies of light when you use it up close. The actual sun feels really warm on your eyes and real bright even at a very low UV. This means your eye doesn't react in a way to cope with it. Also eye burn is caused by uvb so now I just keep the solar glo on my body and use Black lights / ir lights / UVA sun reptile lights / and a 500w halogen bulb within 60cm of my eyes when on computer, no eye burn yet...

### LisaLearning — 2016-04-20T19:12:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193086>

Switch to glasses!

100 years ago, when I started wearing contact lenses, I was told that contact lens use causes photophobia. Nowadays, people are prescribing tinted lenses for photophobia!

I couldn't stand the sun in my eyes after years of wearing contacts, sometimes around the clock. My photophobia is completely gone after ditching contacts and getting actual sun in my eyes, especially the early morning and presunset hours when the sun passes through more of the earth's atmosphere.

I also just had my eyes examined and one of my prescriptions is down 0.75 diopter (and the other is down a tiny amount, but not enough to change my prescription), and I am very myopic. This is the first time my eyes have ever improved since I started wearing glasses at 5.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-04-20T22:52:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193107>

People have forgotten the power of the sun........we focus on just pathology and forget physiologic benefits. They are massive.

### seanb4 — 2016-04-21T17:35:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193165>

I can attest to the power of the sun. Over the last few weeks I've moved houses. On the days were I have been in the garden sun gazing for over 3 hours I have decent sleep (very rare for me), on days I miss out, very poor sleep. My gastroparesis is improved and I swear my eye color has gone from grey to light blue.

My problem is now the weather. It is dog shit where I live, just had 2 sunny days, now its cloudy/rainy and miserable for at least the next 10. I need to combat this as my sleep needs to not be bad.

So I've made some rough calculations. Assuming the UVA intensity at midday is 30 w/m2, I think a 25w blacklight 60cm away gives 0.5 w/m2, meaning you would need 60 Blacklights 60cm away to simulate UVA at noon. Not practical but if you had 5 then 4hrs would be equvalent of 30mins midday sun gazing minus the grounding, and the light being polorised. Most days I can manage this so when I have my lights I will report back on sleep.

I remember Jack saying something like 30% uva gets through on overcast days, so maybe looking in the general direction of the sun on grey days for an hour midday would get you 10 W/m2?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-04-21T18:33:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193176>

12:30 PM today on my DR. FB page will have something about this thread that is important

### seanb4 — 2016-04-22T12:21:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/light-sensitivity-does-it-mean-something.17702/post-193224>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=193176)
>
> 12:30 PM today on my DR. FB page will have something about this thread that is important

" It also implies if you have avoid the sun just jumping out in it might harm your eyes. You need a gradual re exposure to the sun as you add back DHA, water, and solar amplification when you body has been buried from the sun for so long."

I can attest to this too. I spent extra time out in the sun, gazing mostly at it yesterday. Good sleep again however now the center of my left eye has a constant after image of the sun. I figured it would reduce after night but it has not. Be careful and look to the side of the sun if in doubt. Hopefully it will go with icing and time.

# Thread: Green space may make city kids smarter
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-space-may-make-city-kids-smarter.17707/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2016-03-21T21:27:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/green-space-may-make-city-kids-smarter.17707/post-191261>

[http://www.world-science.net/exclusives/150615\_greenspace.htm](http://www.world-science.net/exclusives/150615_greenspace.htm)

Of course, the only thing not mentioned in this article is sunshine...

**Exposure to green spaces may help boost children’s cognitive development, according to a study, which attributed part of the effect to lower pollution.

Researchers monitored changes in cognitive measures every three months between January 2012 and March 2013 among 2,593 grade-school age children ages 7 to 10 in Barcelona, Spain.
**
{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

****Over the course of a year, exposure to greenness within and around schools—as determined by satellite data—was linked with enhanced mental ability to continuously manipulate and update information, according to the investigators. These abilities are called working memory and superior working memory, respectively.

Reduced inattentive was another benefit, according to the study.

The results were independent of ethnicity, maternal education, and parental employment, said the researchers, Payam Dadvand of the Center for Research in Environmental Epidemiology in Barcelona and colleagues.

The results linked every increase in total surrounding greenness by a quartile to a 5 percent increase in working memory, 6 percent increase in superior working memory, and 1 percent reduction in inattentiveness.

A computer modeling analysis suggested that elemental carbon, a common traffic-related air pollutant, might account for 20 percent to 65 percent of the estimated links between school greenness and cognitive development.

No significant link turned up between exposure to greenness at home and cognitive measures, the researchers said. Given the soaring rates of global urbanization, expanding green spaces at schools might be a step toward improving academic attainment among children, according to the authors.

Dadvand and colleagues said previous studies had linked green space to better mental health, but whether this might influence cognitive development specifically was unclear.

“Contact with nature is thought to play a crucial and irreplaceable role in brain development,” the researchers wrote, reporting their findings this week in the early online edition of the research journal _Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences_.

“Natural environments including green spaces provide children with unique opportunities such as inciting engagement, risk taking, discovery, creativity, mastery and control, strengthening sense of self, inspiring basic emotional states including sense of wonder, and enhancing psychological restoration, which are suggested to influence positively different aspects of cognitive development.”****

The reason given for the shortage given was that over 40% of the blood collected in this last month had to be thrown out due to low hemoglobin content of donors. People in Ireland live in a very poor UV region globally and on top of it something happened this year to make it worse. This all occurred right at winter's end and is at the end of our worst winter on record over there; they had over 10 major storms and CONSTANT gloom from their weather data. Do you reckon this lack of sunlight could be responsible for the epidemic of low hemoglobin from donors? Erythropoetin is a solar hormone too people and we need it to avoid anemia to carry UV and IR light from our surfaces to our interiors. Hemoglobin has 4 key frequencies it absorbs in…one UV and 3 in the red……..what does that tell you? Light is my scalpel........always has been. Too bad for the critics that left the building and could not wait for the full harvest of the ideas. Sun light, not food, nature, not modern beliefs burying the sun. Nature's concepts not modern manufactured precepts. Reality trumps your perception of it. Attention follows energy, and the absence of light in proteins tells the sun where to refill us to avoid degeneration. Hemoglobin’s peak light absorption peaks at 4 specific frequencies "280 mm,420nm, 540nm, and 580nm" and has a very sharp cutoff at 600 nm. When you live in Ireland indoors under fake light you never see one of these frequencies of light.......NOT ONE. This points out why blue -green frequency spikes in fake light might be behind many blood disorders. Anemia is just one. These absorption peaks in hemoglobin are ideally match the spectral absorptions of the arteries in skin and the aorta deep inside of us where atherosclerosis predominates. These ideas go way past atherosclerosis and anemia. They reach all the way to neurologic diseases like tumors and to neurodegeneration too because of the effect on the SCN and the RPE and dopamine in the brain as I laid out in Ubiquitination 24 blog and the Time series. Light is the lever for chromosomes. Chromosomes are not the lever of light. Genes are quantum computers and the environment they exist in is their measuring devices. Measurement and observation effects are made by the respiratory protein to control the nuclear gene expression. The genome is an ‘organ of the cell’, not its dictator.

Your labs show what Vancouver is really doing to you........until you own it........it won't change.

### Just Breathe — 2016-05-03T19:12:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-does-our-ability-to-tan-mean.17721/post-194099>

Aerose, I have been where you are. Every little damn thing I did was a huge crash, errors costly, colossal difficulties.
Here is what I did. I told myself that my journey is my own and I am the top priority.
Staying up til 11 induces a 6 week crash? its out.
One smidgen of gluten causes difficulty walking for a month? It's out.
Your job right now is to stay in the tight tunnel that you are on. It is tighter for you than many others. It will not always be this way.
Every step is closer to the light and the tunnel will expand larger and larger as you go. There is no way to anticipate a reaction. Accept them, learn from them, and keep going.
Use that internet meme of what success looks like v. what success really looks like and keep going.
Also, use the salesman's mantra of "No's can actually be "not right now's"
I predict that a year from now, you will be in the sun with a tank top on for more than an hour, with no crashes at all!

# Thread: WHERE DID THE QUANTLET GET ITS IDEAS?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-did-the-quantlet-get-its-ideas.17723/>
**Subforum:** Redox Rx
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-24T18:49:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-did-the-quantlet-get-its-ideas.17723/post-191452>

Previous work assumed that ATP synthase, the smallest known rotary motor in nature, operates at 100% efficiency. Calculations which arrive to this result assume that the water viscosity inside mitochondria is constant and corresponds to that of bulk water. In our opinion this assumption is not satisfactory for two reasons: (1) There is evidence that the water in mitochondria prevails to 100% as interfacial water. (2) Laboratory experiments which explore the properties of interfacial water suggest viscosities which exceed those of bulk water, specifically at hydrophilic interfaces. Here, we wish to suggest a physicochemical mechanism which assumes intramitochondrial water viscosity gradients and consistently explains two cellular responses: The decrease and increase in ATP synthesis in response to reactive oxygen species and non-destructive levels of near-infrared (NIR) laser light, respectively. The mechanism is derived from the results of a new experimental method, which combines the technique of nanoindentation with the modulation of interfacial water layers by laser irradiation. Results, including the elucidation of the principle of light-induced ATP production, are expected to have broad implications in all fields of medicine.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4495567/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4495567/)

The change in density changes water refraction to light to make a battery..........BOOM

### Joe Gavin — 2016-03-25T16:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-did-the-quantlet-get-its-ideas.17723/post-191493>

Awesome. I'm making linkages to the other post on light polarization in water as well, and the scattering of light in water.
"The origin of this pattern is the same as for the celestial pattern; photons are preferentially scattered towards an observer when their e-vectors are perpendicular to the plane passing through the observer, the light source and the point of scattering, and **scattering is particularly favored at right angles**. The pattern is **actually more stable in water** than in air for a given solar angle, since the light in air can be highly directional in clear skies and nearly omnidirectional under clouds, but underwater at moderate depths, the region of greatest brightness will always be near the refracted position of the sun in the sky, although the distribution of brightness will always be broad due to in-water scattering. Consequently, maximum polarization in water will be reduced compared with that in clear skies in air but greater than under cloudy skies in air, and the overall pattern of polarization is likely to be relatively insensitive to sky state except under heavy overcast (when the pattern should come to resemble that of Fig. 3). In his pioneering work, Waterman (Waterman, 1954) noted that the celestial polarization pattern has little influence on submarine polarization at depths exceeding a few meters. Our results support this conclusion; **there is little or no suggestion of differences in the polarization pattern inside Snell’s window** compared with the field as a whole. Thus, the patterns of polarization computed by Horváth and Varjú (Horváth and Varjú, 1995) for the situation in shallow water under a flat surface do not appear here. The loss of the atmospheric pattern is certainly due to its **destruction by multiple-path scattering**; in fact, Waterman (Waterman, 1954) noted that the atmospheric pattern is visible at depths of 2–3 m in very shallow and clear marine waters. Finally, **polarization in water is surprisingly insensitive to wavelength"**

Cool shit as usual. Sure April will be huge.

### Lin Moore — 2016-03-24T21:14:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone.17724/post-191463>

I'm needing help with my labs. My progesterone is low and my testosterone is high. (Which I understand now after reading hours and hours on the blog).

My twin sister (also with hashimotos and pcos) had the same labs done at a different provider and we are confused on ranges.

She was told her estrogen was high and testosterone was normal. However, I was told normal range was under 65 for testosterone - and mine is 85.

She was told normal range was under 82 for testosterone - hers is 40.

The practitioner told me to stop taking supplemental testosterone. I told her she must be confused, I've never taken any!!!

I've read Epi Paleo 2x, and working hard at getting am light, eating high protein and dha rich breakfast and lowering blue light exposure. I started red light therapy. I'm normal weight with a leptin of 12.

Is there anything I can do immediately to help with the symptoms of high testosterone? And, what really is the cut off for the lab level?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-31T17:48:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone.17724/post-191782>

High T = too much blue light and not enough UV. Anna cabeca podcast I did and this:

These data contained in this paper demonstrate that the environment can dictate how hormones affect a complex behavior by altering the molecular pathways targeted by steroid receptors. Why is it that no one discusses this? Is it because our healthcare advice only discusses the pathologic aspects of sunlight and never discusses the natural physiology of how sunlight affects hormonal regulation via the eye and skin. Sunlight makes our surfaces relative, and that relativity changes the space/time of our cells and tissues. It shifts blood into the skin by dermal pooling due to eNOS and NO. More than 50% of your blood volume can change your skin surface when sun is present. This can lower your blood pressure. It changes the anatomical structure of the skin by changing the optics of surfaces. It becomes a sensory stimulus for the interoceptive system to induce biochemical substrates via photosynthesis in plants and changes in the ATPase using red light and water in animals. Sunlight increase Vitamin D, histamine, and sulfhydryl groups, while lowering (photolysis) adrenalin, steroids, testosterone, estrogen, thyroid hormone, DNA and RNA. Sunlight induces biochemical reactions via photolysis and it induces coordinated endocrine adaptation effects in the eye and the skin surfaces. It affects the sympathetic and parasympathetic systems. It is the stimulus for the circadian timing mechanism of the body clock via the central retinal pathways.

Melatonin is an antagonist of aromatase so it lowers estrogen in tissues. PCOS in women and high E2 levels in men are signs of sunlight deficiency and a low quantum yield environment. Left untreated, this will cause % heteroplasmy to increase and insulin resistance to rise, glucose control loss, loss of sulfation of the blood plasma, and eventual metabolic syndrome and a change of oxidize lipids with higher mass and inertia in the blood. The answer is not blood pressure medicine, statins, and glucophage…….it is sensible sun exposure. The sun makes melatonin. [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/17525148](http://l.facebook.com/l.php?u=http%3A%2F%2Fwww.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov%2Fpubmed%2F17525148&h=QAQEgZZ6XAQEI3vZEZXPAUG-xeos4JfEGeSMb7-xHx1l-3w&enc=AZPYKj_h7DC3Md6XF6q-KGh2k6jg5e4ZBZ4uGBb9BNrcRv3VCRKYef8o3NwKRRneEsO1Yd-saE9jz_qnVoZ3DXc2__NcbK-Go21iKtITvdOisDTEaacXQtvtvGbOfdoeDq77eLSI2Cb9NTIBxob1-g2cx5obSMVwLzvdU6vYc92kZPTFRsCrUH5h1ALl_onYg0sksOHV5h1eQxnwJkhN5zWS&s=1)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-31T17:51:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone.17724/post-191783>

Red light slows time. Cold slows time. Blue light speeds up time and your life is lived like a blue straggler star. Quick and bright. Over supplemented comes from being undereducated about light. Light has weight. As it falls to anything with gravity it gets heavier because light has momentum. Light moving away from mass allows light to "get lighter" because it loses its purple power and shifts to red. Gravitational lensing is a redshift effect.

Sunlight……its light shrinks your respiratory proteins to make your Nissan Sentra a Ferrari.

### lohd2015 — 2016-04-02T22:38:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-testosterone.17724/post-191920>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=191783)
>
> Red light slows time. Cold slows time. Blue light speeds up time and your life is lived like a blue straggler star. Quick and bright. Over supplemented comes from being undereducated about light. Light has weight. As it falls to anything with gravity it gets heavier because light has momentum. Light moving away from mass allows light to "get lighter" because it loses its purple power and shifts to red. Gravitational lensing is a redshift effect.
>
> Sunlight……its light shrinks your respiratory proteins to make your Nissan Sentra a Ferrari.

Two questions please:

1\. Red light shrinks respiratory proteins = higher pH = lower inflammation. I cannot agree more.

However, I think I overdid my IR red light (640nm-660nm LED bulb) sessions last week. I did 1.5 hours on two sunless days in a row (also did not turn on UV bulb as it was before 10 am). I felt fantastic. Aches all gone (especially my right leg which bothered me since I was 7). But I noticed a huge drop in cold temperature tolerance and I was sniffling non-stop. (I stopped sniffling on a regular basis three years ago). The sniffling continued for about 5 days even after I stopped all IR sessions. I was only back to normal after 2 hours of sungazing this morning and one hour of nude sun bathing (UV index 5.1 to 7.4 between 11 to 12). Now that my sniffling has stopped and my cold tolerance is back to where it was, my right leg is aching again (no aches during the days when I was sniffling).

So the question is: Too much IR without enough UV to charge the battery = no good?

2\. At the end of the video "Why the Sun is Necessary for Optimal Health", Dr. Wunsch says the heat we feel on the skin is from UV. Did I get that wrong?

# Thread: Finding my way back to optimal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 82
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Burning Star — 2016-03-24T23:33:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-191465>

Mid 30s, living in Toronto. I'm convsidering moving north on the Georgian Bay, away from the Toronto nnEMF and within 30 minutes of Emvale Spring. From Nov to end of April I will be living in South Florida. I’ve been here since early December.

**Symptoms:**

Hypothyroidism – not hashimoto’s
Psoriasis
mild Crohns – in remission
Dermatitis

Lack of energy
Insomnia – trouble falling asleep mostly trouble staying asleep
tired but wired at night
impaired short-term memory
Loss of excitement, pleasure feelings
Loss of ambition, motivation
Poor digestion
anxiety
Depression
Difficulty thinking / concentrating
Restlessness - impatient
take things too seriously and easily defensive
Floaters
cervical tightness from inflammation, not muscle tension

**Lab tests:**

Sept 2014: (before stopping synthroid)

**Total T3:** 1.3 .......1.0-2.7 nmol/L

I could last so much longer at the lake without sweating as compared to the park. Made 15,000+ IUs of vit D according to dminder. Wish the lake was colder....

### Burning Star — 2016-06-09T00:39:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-196188>

Purchased a 3" firm latex topper to use over my 20 gauss magnetico to give me an extra 5 gauss and I can notice an improvement in sleep. Andrea of magnetico confirmed that using a 3" firm topper is the lowest one could likely use over the booster without creating a positive field. Didn't really feel like going back to bed after waking. I tried for like 5 minutes but couldn't fall asleep. Instead I went across the street to the park to watch the sun rise and do a little tai chi in the chilly air.

In fact, didn't feel like napping at all today and I got no natural vit D today at all, just the black light. I ate my usual daily dose of 24 raw oysters, some fresh herring in vinegar, as well as fresh anchovies.

It's 50 degrees here in Toronto and chilly. Headed off to the lake.

### Burning Star — 2016-07-02T03:14:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-197724>

Some observations:

If I spend a day without sun, I feel like hell. I'll even getting mild palpitations. On the days I do get sun, if it’s not enough, I feel brain-dead after around 5pm. I slept so much better in Florida even without doing CT and eating a lot less DHA. I've been eating 24 oysters almost every day since around April 20

0.5mg of nicotine every 3-4 hrs seems to work well for me. Although there’s no buzz, it seems to help keep me in parasympathetic mode. If I take 2mg within a short period of time, I get an uncomfortable buzz that makes me more brain fogged than focused. I have zero cravings for nicotine if I don’t take it for long periods of time.

### Burning Star — 2016-07-04T13:59:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-197836>

Thought I should update my first post since I jumped right to my labs without giving a proper introduction.

Ever since I was a child I’ve found myself easily getting into the “fight or flight” mode. I remember getting depressed at times but my family remembers me as being very happy. This next part is quite embarrassing but I’m sure is useful information: I was a bed wetter till I was 12 years old which may have been a result of my anxiety. Whenever my parents woke me up during the night, either to help change the sheets or remind me to pee the lights were on.. Falling asleep was always an issue for me, in general and I moved a lot in my sleep. Sometimes I ended up at the other end of the bed or on the floor when I woke the next morning. However, when I did sleep, I was a vivid dreamer. Always dreaming. Sometime even during the day. When I was in grade two or three, my teacher mentioned to my parents how I would stare out the window instead of paying attention. Being concerned, my parents had me tested. The psychologist explained that there was nothing wrong and that I had the potential to go into whatever profession I desired. However, even as a child, I had a hard time concentrating and paying attention, and experienced frequent brain fog. There were even times when I would burst out in uncontrollable anger at my older brother. I was frequently moody. Even in my early 20s, while in university, whenever I took the train home I was very moody upon arrival. Fortunately, I went to overnight camp from 7-13 years old where I always came back dark brown. As well, we often took vacations to California 3-5 times a year where I would tan very quickly there too. Never applied sunscreen before my early 20s and when I did start using it, it was very sparingly.

I was hospitalized for Pancreatitis in 98. However, the pancreatitis went away on its own a few days later.

That's where I'll leave it as the story gets much more complicated. There was something in particular that occurred in the year 2000 that destroyed my mitochondria that I’m still trying to recover from, but I can’t share those details on the forum.

Some background on my mom. My mother, born in Toronto, had two miscarriages before having my older brother. Once her doc put her on progesterone, she was able to get pregnant. She was chubby till her early 20s. Her mom was born in Minnesota and moved to Winnipeg when she was very young. Other than being hypothyroid and having conventional refractive cataract surgery, my mom has not been diagnosed with anything else.

I’m looking forward to reading the blogs once I land in Mexico in September. I’ve been busy prepping for my 8 month trip since May and so really haven’t had time for much deep reading.

### Burning Star — 2016-07-08T00:12:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-198032>

haplogroups:

Maternal Line: U7 (which is a subgroup of R)
Haplogroup R arose in southwest Asia not long after humans first expanded beyond Africa. It eventually spread throughout Eurasia, giving rise to most of the major haplogroups of Europe as well as one – haplogroup B – that was involved in the migration of people from Siberia into the Americas.

-

    -   **Haplogroup:** R, a subgroup of N

    -   **Age:** 60,000 years

    -   **Region:** Global

    -   **Example Populations:** Indians,Semai, Daic

    -   **Highlight:** Several of the major European haplogroups arose from R.

> haplogroups:

> Maternal Line: U7 (which is a subgroup of R)
> Haplogroup R arose in southwest Asia not long after humans first expanded beyond Africa. It eventually spread throughout Eurasia, giving rise to most of the major haplogroups of Europe as well as one – haplogroup B – that was involved in the migration of people from Siberia into the Americas.
>

>     -   **Haplogroup:** R, a subgroup of N
>
>     -   **Age:** 60,000 years
>
>     -   **Region:** Global
>
>     -   **Example Populations:** Indians,Semai, Daic
>
>     -   **Highlight:** Several of the major European haplogroups arose from R.
>

>
> Paternal Line: G2c (which is a subgroup of G)
> Haplogroup G appears to have arisen in the Caucasus region during the Ice Age, about 16,000 years ago. The high mountains blocked progress of the glaciers that were covering most of Europe and Asia, allowing a refuge for humans and animals. Haplogroup G is still common in the Caucasus region (including Armenia, Georgia, and Azerbaijan) and in the neighboring Near East. Men expanding out of the Near East carried G across much of southern Europe and northern Africa. A separate, smaller migration carried G eastward, where it is seen in low frequencies in groups as far east as China.
>
>
> -   **Haplogroup:** G, a subgroup of F
> -   **Age:** 16,000 years
> -   **Region:** Central Asia, Near East, Northern Africa, Europe
> -   **Example Populations:** Palestinians, Ossetians, Georgians, Moroccans
> -   **Highlight:** Haplogroup G probably originated in the Caucasus but made its way across the Near East, northern Africa, southern Europe, and even into China.

### Burning Star — 2016-08-14T00:01:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-199591>

It was a cloudy / rainy day today so I decided to try some oral USP MB that was compounded for me by the pharmacy. At 50mcg, I didn't really notice an effect. Perhaps it was too subtle. Later in the afternoon I took another 50mcg. My mood definitely brightened, I became more gregarious, and my energy increased (almost too much). During my reflexology treatment shortly after my 2nd dose I couldn't fall asleep, even though I normally do. I attribute this to the increase in dopamine. Will save MB for those times that I don't get a lot of sun. Since MB worked, I gather this means I have a leaked BB barrier, which is certainly not a good thing.

### Burning Star — 2016-08-21T00:28:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-199930>

Saw my acupuncturist today, 3 weeks after my last treatment, and he mentioned how my nervous system has improved dramatically. He did an aggressive full back treatment that invigorated me, whereas before I would feel tired with even a much weaker treatment. As an experiment, my acupuncturist sprayed the meridian points with colloidal silver before applying the needles and moxa in order to improve conductivity. Been doing lots of sun lately but no CT. Did not use any other mitochondria boosters today like nicotine or MB.

### Burning Star — 2016-10-01T03:50:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-202149>

Been in PDC for almost 17 days now and energy has improved along with sleep. Today was really the first day that I spent more than usual (since being in PDC) in front of my PC, albeit with 550 BPI shades on as well as an amber filter over my screen. Although I did spend some time outside making about 6K ius of vit D but hardly any time in AM staring in the direction of the sun, my energy dropped. I notice that I can spend hours in the sun, mostly floating in the ocean, making more than 20K ius according to dminder, yet not burn. The skin below my eyes were a little red yesterday but it was gone the next day, perhaps with the help of CT face dunks at night and eating lots of sulfur, shrimp, and raw oysters daily. I guess I really need that sun as I seem to soak it up and feel noticeably worse when I don't spend enough time under direct sunlight.

I'm purchasing a commercial ice machine along with a custom water filter for my apartment as I'll be here for 5 years. Can't wait to start doing whole body CT at night as I do well with CT. I've been doing face dunks in the interim.

### Burning Star — 2016-10-02T22:50:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-202212>

As I try to do every day since being in my new home, woke up this morning to watch the sunrise at the beach, ate my high protein BAB, went for a swim, floated nude to get sun over my entire body. Went home at 8am (as there is no where to go to the bathroom where I am at the beach), bathed nude on my deck till 11am, went bach to ocean to float nude till 2pm. Holly crap, I feel charged!. Never spent so much time in sich a high yield environment under the sun in the water nude for so long. I get the same feeling when I take low dose MB and / or nicotine.

Sleep last night was surprisingly the best it's been in a very long time. I want to say 25 years. However, my standards for sleep are pretty low since it's been trashed for so long. I think it was too soon to introduce carbs as that is what I cut out 3 days ago - basically all fruit. I was eating bananas and mangos every day but I guess I wan't ready for them yet. I kept the veggies, though.

Going to start 16 hour fasts from 7am - 3pm, trying to make sure I get 12 big as oysters in by 3pm.

### lohd2015 — 2016-10-13T19:05:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-202752>

For those biohacking in Mexico, please be reminded that the government there adds FLUORIDE to almost all commercially available salts, unless the salt is imported. Most salt labeling will show the addition of Fluoride. If you eat out, chances are you are getting fluoridated salt. Fluoride is a dielectric blocker.
This is from Doc's post on FB on 8-16-16

"Last year on May 1st 2015, more than 200 scientists worldwide expressed concern about highly fluorinated chemicals - commonly found in non-stick cookware and food packaging. Fluorinated chemicals effect water chemistry of things within that cooking utensil. The higher the temperature the more fluoride is liberated into the foods and affect the water within the foods. Water has a high dielectric constant. It is 78 in bulk water, to be exact, Why is that critical? How much do you know about semiconductor integrated circuits? In a typical network in an integrated circuit, each network will include at least one driver, which must contain a source or drain diffusion and at least one receiver. This set up will consist of a gate electrode over a thin gate dielectric (look for a view of a MOS transistor on line if you’re unsure of this to get a visual.)

What did that all just mean? Did I just describe what a cytochrome 1 really does in your mitochondria for you? Yes I did. Fluoride shorts cytochrome semiconductors NADH/NAD+ out. This is relevant because capacitance (ability to store charge) is directly proportional to dielectric constant of a capacitor. Remember things containing fluoride destroy the dielectric constant in water in foods. This lowers the EZ it can build in your cells. The higher the dielectric constant is, the more charge can be stored so the higher battery function it has and the less ATP you need to generate. When water is charge separated into an exclusion zone the dielectric constant rises to 160 from 78. This makes EZ water the ultimate place to store the charges that the sun’s light deliver’s to Earth. Are you beginning to see why Pollack’s work on water is pretty important yet? Cells are designed to hold massive amounts of negative charge because of the EZ in water is built from UV/IR light. DHA provides the electrons to capture the sun's light source as electrons are excited to build that EZ in cells. This is why it is the critical part of the antenna complex in cell membranes in mammals.

In the ubiquitination series, I showed you a loss of cell signaling in cytochrome 1, means redox becomes reduced at cytochrome 1 and lowers superoxide burst and affects cardiolipin release. We lose the ability to pulse superoxide and pseudohypoxia is the result. What are the implications of losing this pulsed superoxide? You lose the ability to collect and move electrons in the brain’s neurons using DHA. This means the mitochondria in these neurons cannot harvest those electrons of their light energy. One of the most interesting features of electrons is their spin and how they change in mitochondria. All free radicals made in mitochondria have unpair electrons. When an electron is bound to a semiconductor like cytochrome 1 and its electron spins are paired, it loses its spin and magnetism and like photons, only acts like a particle and cannot act as a wave. When electrons are bound to a semiconductor they can only act like a particle and not as a wave. This means cytochromes accept the kinetic energy of the electron and begin to vibrate. Most people do not know about these non linear states found in a semiconducting electric currents because they do not understand the physics. This queer electric feature is very useful to life in mitochondria. In this way, none of the stored light energy in an electron in a cells lattice can be thermalized. This means that an excited or ground state electron that is paired in mitochondria, cannot generate any heat if it is stored in our tissues in this way. When the electron is not paired a free radical is made and light photons are liberated from the excited electron. As a result, your O2 levels drop in your mitochondria and pseudohypoxia is the result. Do not forget in cell membranes DHA and water meet everywhere in the brain next to that CSF lake. There is a deep reason for this. This is how an antenna works in the human brain. This is how electrons are transferred from an outside source to inside repository for signaling."

D-ribose works very well for me cause when I stop taking it, energy plummets.

Arrived back to Play yesterday after a week in Aruba. My friends in PDC commented on how much more tanned I was than when I left. I'm considering moving to Costa Rica next Fall and Winter to maximize the effect from the sun and then PDC for spring and summer. Some hacks I'm going to implement.

Upping my carb intake based on my solar exposure to optimize sleep
Applying 90W 660 and 630 UFO light to my weak areas from 3pm - sundown
Connecting with a traditional Chinese medicine doc to learn how to perform acupuncture on myself while shining acupuncture red lasers on those points.
Renting a place with a pool in May so I can ground and CT at the same time

### Burning Star — 2017-06-29T04:04:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-217400>

It's been a while since my last update. On the positive note, when I went back to Toronto in early March my TCM practitioner commented how my nervous system, "liver pulse" (30% improvement), and Adrenals have all strengthened. Overall, though, I still feel like crap - tired all time, brain fog, poor memory, no drive to thrive. He mentioned that the pulses will improve way before I will really notice the effects. Based on labs I did in Toronto BUN / Creatinine ratio lowered to 12.5 and vitamin D is 80.

Back in April I couldn’t stay out in the sun for very long without experiencing shortness of breath, nausea, dizziness...After going back to s Pellegrino, adding sea salt and more limes to my water, and spending time in the ocean in early AM to improve electrolytes, I felt much better.

Iron seemed fairly low based on my last labs so I started eating chicken liver several times a week. Switched to raw, grass fed / finished beef liver while in Toronto.

B12 was a little low, as well. Hadn’t had any red meat from September to March and my No2 production was very high so that seems to be why B12 and iron may not be optimal. Since my return to Toronto on June 15, I’ve been eating venison regularly.

Trying to source oysters in PDC as I had been eating them daily till mid October, then the restaurant decided to take them off the menu. In Toronto I’ve been eating 24 oysters daily as well as regularly eating shrimp / lobster stock mixed with bone broth.

Noticed my teeth were less sensitive at my last hygienist visit last week which I believe is a sign of improved redox. This is interesting since I’ve been taking worse care of my teeth for the last 6 months.

Just before leaving for Toronto this time, I noticed sleep improve for the 1st time in a while and I had to use a belt to keep my shorts up. Someone also asked if I had been working out which I have not but interesting none the less.

Been experimenting with using IR light (bcure device) with my TCM practitioner. Using stainless steel needles, we experiment by shining the light on them before use and / or shining the light on the meridian point with the needle is already in.

Been using the quantlet on relax or wellness mode in the AM. It seems to improve my mood, especially when I don’t get much AM sunlight.

Getting ready to go to Ecuador before my dopamine sinks too low and I back out. Will be back in Playa in September where I'll be renting a house in the jungle!

> Been experimenting with using IR light (bcure device) with my TCM practitioner. Using stainless steel needles, we experiment by shining the light on them before use and / or shining the light on the meridian point with the needle is already in.
>
> Been using the quantlet on relax or wellness mode in the AM. It seems to improve my mood, especially when I don’t get much AM sunlight.
>
> Getting ready to go to Ecuador before my dopamine sinks too low and I back out. Will be back in Playa in September where I'll be renting a house in the jungle!

What is your experience with IR light and acupuncture needles? Too soon to tell? Any difference? Some acupuncturist will light up the end of the needle with a small clump of herbs. Heat = IR, although the herbs probably dictate the frequencies.
Thank you for sharing.

### Burning Star — 2017-06-29T04:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-217404>

We just started experimenting today with IR light. We worked on the adrenal meridians. Tomorrow he will asses the effects based on my subjective response and his physical examination of my back where the needles and light were used.

Lighting up the end of the needle is a form of moxibustion. Yes, moxa does = IR light.

My TCM practitioner uses that under certain circumstances among the many tools he has in his arsenal.

### lohd2015 — 2017-06-29T04:28:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-217408>

> [Burning Star said:](/goto/post?id=217404)
>
> We just started experimenting today with IR light. We worked on the adrenal meridians. Tomorrow he will asses the effects based on my subjective response and his physical examination of my back where the needles and light were used.
>
> Lighting up the end of the needle is a form of moxibustion. Yes, moxa does = IR light.
>
> My TCM practitioner uses that under certain circumstances among the many tools he has in his arsenal.

Acupuncture actually evolved from moxibustion. The chinese term for acupuncture is comprised of two words 針灸, 針 for needle, and 灸 for the act of heating up something. The method of 灸 was first described in Huang Di Nei Jing, around 2600 BC. The original method which PREDATED Huang Di involved the use of hot stones.

### Burning Star — 2017-10-10T00:53:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finding-my-way-back-to-optimal.17725/post-225404>

As I did not feel like I was making any gains in Ecuador, I decided to head back to PDC after only 6 days. One of my PDC friends mentioned how much more tanned I was from only a month earlier. I guess it was all that strong UVA light I was getting all day in my kiniki, if not naked, grounded on the yard, even while working on my laptop. At around solor noon I was making 500 iu of D / minute.

While in PDC I notice that I can only handle about 20K iu of D + about 100g of carbs from fruit, otherwise I get too agitated if I push either the UVB or the carbs, or both. I'm going to experiment with the Bcure on my adrenals to see if this allows me to stay out longer in the afternoon.

I’ve noticed that getting UV light into my eyes is way more important than getting it on the skin, perhaps since my sun callous is so strong.

When I returned to Toronto at the end of August everyone was commenting on how much more grounded I seemed since my visit in June. That surprised me since it was only a couple months back. I guess I've been able to capture and store more electrons, and therefore photons.

Based on labs from end of August my bun/ creatinine is now below 10, vit D is right at the top of the range, and I'm no longer B12 deficient. Iron still seems low , Thyroid and DHEA are horrible.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-25T00:18:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-what-does-it-imply.17727/post-191467>

Tinnitus is from a brain's mitochondria in the brainstem and acoustic radiations telling the owner of said brain the environment they are currently in is suboptimal and they should move. The ringing is the brain's way of signaling the animal it is trying to heal itself by creating phonons to raise the DC electric current in th brain to increase the local melatonin levels to shrink the respiratory proteins. Wild animals listen to their intuition and move and modern humans cannot fathom they need to move and instead go to the ENT doctors and get meds that don't work and remain in those environments. That is why so people in population dense areas are getting tinnitus. Enjoy, your cell phone, wifi, and laptops in you coffee shops and street corners in your major urban centers. And don't lean up against a wall with an outlet. AC currents really drive a brain nuts. You can never get well in the same environment you got chronically ill within, says, Mother Nature.

[http://www.bbc.com/news/science-env...e=facebook&ns\_linkname=knowledge\_and\_learning](http://www.bbc.com/news/science-environment-32414876?ns_mchannel=social&ns_campaign=BBC_iWonder&ns_source=facebook&ns_linkname=knowledge_and_learning)

### enyaw — 2017-02-24T18:35:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-what-does-it-imply.17727/post-209243>

What about tinnitus from damage to the ear drum? After spending some years in the Marines, I have tinnitus. Is there a chance that the almost constant tinnitus is from the damage? I do notice that it's worse in certain circumstances. I assume that when it flares up it is in response to higher nnEMF levels in my environment and that the constant ringing is due to a damaged ear drum. Can a damaged ear drum be healed to no longer present tinnitus or is it permanent?

### OptimalSkr — 2017-02-24T20:40:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tinnitus-what-does-it-imply.17727/post-209248>

# Thread: Historical Medical/Health Statistics
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rubicon — 2016-03-25T04:11:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-191480>

Very often Dr Kruse cites statistics like certain cancers were rare several decades ago. I remember listening to a webinar awhile ago that listed off all kinds of interesting stats. Does anyone remember which one that was?

My wife doesn't believe that disease incidence rates has changed much historically, and I'd like to research the topic myself. I believe that one source Dr Kruse has cited were historical records from a medical library.

A bunch of googling for "historical cancer rates", "cancer rate in 1800", or "incidence of stroke 1800", etc doesn't really turn up anything. There was a bit of a controversy in 2010 about absence of evidence of cancer in mummies:

-   Some say [it's rare to find evidence of cancer in Egyptian Mummies](http://phys.org/news/2010-10-scientists-cancer-purely-man-made.html), implying cancer was rare.
-   Others say [cancer was there all along, we just didn't look](http://www.nytimes.com/2010/12/28/health/28cancer.html?_r=1).

Has anyone tried to find historical disease statistics online and had success finding anything?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-25T14:42:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-191489>

1900 Colon cancer was 37th. In 5 generations it is now number 2. US dept of statistics. Your wife needs to up her game

### Rubicon — 2016-03-27T17:34:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-191584>

Re-listening to the [Anna Cabeca podcast](http://podcasts.cabecahealth.com/why-indoors-living-and-cell-phones-can-both-be-bad-for-your-health/), I found one of the quotes I was thinking about. At 01:41:41

JK: When I was at LSU...I got to meet Alton Oshner and he told me the story that he brought medical students in the 1920's to see cases of lung cancer because you may never see these again. That's how rare they were. So when he told me that, I went back and looked in the archives of where I did my residency which is the Charity Hospital which is now closed--we have the best medical records going back to the 1850's--I found out that in the 1900's that colon cancer was the 37th leading cause of cancer. Today it's #2

Interesting, but the source is not accessible online unfortunately.
Searching at the [US Department of Statistics](https://www.usa.gov/statistics) sofar hasn't led to anything. However, I did find some interesting charts at [Cancer.org's 2013 Statistics presentation](http://www.cancer.org/research/cancerfactsstatistics/cancerfactsfigures2013/cancer-statistics-2013-slide-presentation.pdf):

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[](http://imgur.com/j02GvlJ)

​

There's clearly a bit of an uptrend in overall cancer incidence in the US over the last 35 years, but less than I would have expected. I was surprised to see that the colon cancers are falling, but not surprised to see thyroid increasing.
[Here is the report the above charts are based on: Annual Report to the Nation on the Status of Cancer, 1975-2011](http://jnci.oxfordjournals.org/content/107/6/djv048.full?sid=c56f408a-1b77-425f-b785-9aa68fe02f6c)

Another source I came across was cancerresearchuk.org Some results from the UK:​

-   [Cancer Incidence for all cancers](http://www.cancerresearchuk.org/health-professional/cancer-statistics/incidence/all-cancers-combined#heading-One)
-   [Bowel Cancer Incidence](http://www.cancerresearchuk.org/health-professional/cancer-statistics/statistics-by-cancer-type/bowel-cancer/incidence#heading-Two)
-   [Breast Cancer Incidence](http://www.cancerresearchuk.org/health-professional/cancer-statistics/statistics-by-cancer-type/breast-cancer/incidence-invasive#heading-Two)

Interesting that cancer rates in males have been mostly flat since 1990. Cancer rates in women are up much more overall...though they started from a lower incidence rate in the first place.

**Comparing US vs UK Results**
Cancer rates per American in 2011: Males 525, Females 400
Cancer rates per Brits in 2011: Males 673, Females 535
It's unclear if these two data sets are directly comparable, but if cancer rates are generally higher in the UK, perhaps it is partly a light story.

Still looking for similar data that goes back to 1900 or earlier for cancers and other diseases. Even estimated incidence rates would be nice to see.

What will happen to these charts up to 2020 and beyond as decades of elevated nnEMF exposure will have taken place?

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-27T22:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-191598>

you get T2D and AD and PD before cancer...........so I expect to those go through the roof first

### Josh Rosenthal — 2016-04-05T21:14:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-192125>

quick search found this...

[http://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/dvs/lead1900\_98.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/dvs/lead1900_98.pdf)

### Rubicon — 2016-04-05T22:44:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-192129>

> [Josh Rosenthal said:](/goto/post?id=192125)
>
> quick search found this...
> [http://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/dvs/lead1900\_98.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/nchs/data/dvs/lead1900_98.pdf)

It seems there are more statistics available for death rates of disease than there are of incidence rates. The 'problem' is that through increased health care we've been able to reduce most death rates while masking the fact that incidence rates are increasing. If what Dr Kruse is saying is correct, the data seems to be showing that we're getting better and better at clearing weeds as they grow, but there are more and more weeds and we're not really addressing the root of the problem.

For example, via [here](http://www.publichealthreviews.eu/show/f/23), it seems death rates for heart disease and stroke are falling:

Via [here](http://www.rationaloptimist.com/blog/cancer-chemicals-carson-and-smoking/), most death rates from cancer are also falling:

I bet Sam Milham has lots of data like that... he's an epidemiologist who tied the electrification of the states to all modern illnesses...

### Rubicon — 2016-12-11T21:03:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/historical-medical-health-statistics.17728/post-205163>

> There was a bit of a controversy in 2010 about absence of evidence of cancer in mummies:

Sept 2016 webinar @54:05:

-   Ancient Egyptians considered light skin more beautiful than dark skin. Most Egyptian slaves were dark-skinned Nubians. Culture drove Egyptians to attempt to maintain light skin
-   The sun-drenched environment of Egypt made if difficult to prevent tanning
-   Egyptologists have found papayri that indicate Egyptians used potions and sunscreens to keep skin from becoming tanned...particularly the rich rulers that could afford potions/sunscreens
-   Slaves in Egypt were relatively healthier than their masters, the ruling class developed diseases of a modified solar spectrum, the ruling class were mummified, we find diseases like cancer in mummies today

Pretty cool idea. So much good stuff in that webinar...

# Thread: Starting out with CT

### Dead Can Dance — 2016-03-25T07:18:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/starting-out-with-ct.17729/post-191483>

Hiyas,

I've been doing cold water, face dunks and cold showers. At first it was a struggle but it's getting easier.

I wanted to know, instead of cold showers.. can I replace it with jumping in the bath (no water) and throwing a big bag of ice on me (Compression shirt too) ?
Or do most of you go for the ice vests ?

TIA.

### HopingMom — 2016-03-26T17:34:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191552>

Hello all! I apologize if this is a dumb question, but I am just trying to understand this all...
Is it correct that the eyes will have more melanin if they feel they are in trouble to help protect themselves (see this by darker eye/iris color and/or easily sunburned)? So in turn, the good sunlight that you are getting may not be totally utilized as it should? Would then removing the trouble (blue light, light after sunset etc) help to reverse the excess melanin? Anything else not obvious that could contribute to the excess melanin?

Also, wondering if it is possible to increase physical dopamine more on a mental level by removing restrictions, increasing options that kind of thing...basically having more psychological maybe's?

Lastly, wondering if anyone has any personal experience or thoughts on iridology in relation to all of this or health in general?

TIA for your time and help!

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-26T18:42:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191553>

dark colors absorb light faster but also release it quicker to surroundings........

### Sue-UK — 2016-03-27T15:38:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191581>

> [HopingMom said:](/goto/post?id=191552)
>
> Lastly, wondering if anyone has any personal experience or thoughts on iridology in relation to all of this or health in general?

Hi HopingMom

I trained in iridology over 20 years ago, so have looked at a few irises . Not done much with it for about 5 years though, and almost none since I started Krusing.  I remember on the beginners practical course, I was the only one in the class who had a particular sign in my eyes,  but by the time I went back for the advanced practical 6 months later the sign had gone, after I had used naturopathic treatment. However back then lifestyle, technology and light pollution was nothing like it is now, and I don't think the same naturopathic treatment I used works in the same way now.I feel the same about homeopathy too.

My brother went to live in Spain a few years back. He left the UK with very hazel eyes, took 6 months off and lived in what was basically a hut on a small campsite close to the beach. Spent his days sunbathing, walking on the beach, and he gave up alcohol, drank loads of water, and lived off seafood, meat and salads mainly, but no junk food. When I saw him after the 6 months, his eyes were blue. The difference was startling. Back then I put it down more to relaxing, better diet, no alcohol, water etc, i.e. conventional naturopathics, but now I think light, water, magnetism was the primary influence.... He then went back to living in a town, working (a lot of which is technology based), wearing sunglasses more, a more typical diet and drinking beer, less water, and over time his eyes returned to being hazel. The environment his mitochondria faced changed, and so did his eye colour.

### HopingMom — 2016-03-27T16:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191582>

Hi Sue-Uk,
We actually did a reading for my son and many of his symptoms seemed to match up to the reading...at first I was a bit skeptical, however there were a few things that were reported that didn't fit any of our health history (that we gave to the practitioner), but we knew were there...so I did a bit of a "holy moly this is for real". That is an amazing story about your brother.
Something I haven't been able to understand is why is all of that sulfur building up in the GI tract? My son is supposed to have blue eyes, but they are actually dark hazel and the highest concentration is in the GI tract. Now I learn a bit more from Dr K and great minds like yours and things are starting to click into place...

### Sue-UK — 2016-03-27T17:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191583>

Hi HopingMom,
The problem I have with iridology is that too much focus can be put on a particular sign ....So X sign means this, and to clear it take remedy Y..... but if X wasn't caused by lack of remedy Y, that's when time and money are wasted ..... for example in my experience with iridology, "toxicity" is usually "worse" round the GI tract, or can appear to originate from it (the brain being poisoned by a toxic gut was the sign I had when I did my first course). I think of the body as a flow system,my brothers hazel eyes cleared to blue naturally, it didn't matter if it was sulfur or anything else, he was unknowingly getting all his circadian rhythms (including his gut) in sync, harvesting light and electrons, drinking lots of water, experiencing the earth's magnetic field, the Schumann resonance .... All at the right time. Thats where the magic started ......

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-27T21:50:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191595>

everything is a sliding scale when you alter your environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-27T22:06:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191596>

Now I will say the following: claims made by the practitioner's are not supportive...........by data but the data they should be using is % heteroplasmy which we still cannot measure well but Ruben and I are working on that aspect.

To see if this is pseudoscience someone is going to need to use a quantum detector........why?

It has been pointed out that the premise of iridology is at odds with the fact that the iris does not undergo substantial changes in an individual's life. Iris texture is a [phenotypical](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Phenotypical) feature that develops during [gestation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gestation) and remains unchanged after birth.

Why dont I just hammer iridology like everyone else?

Doug Wallace. He has shown that phenotypes of diseases can manifest when %heteroplasmy changes in mitochondria. My bet is that iris changes are so subtle that our current techniques would never pick them up but the water around an mitochondria can do this and in turn it will affect the respiratory proteins in the mitochondria.

Frequency change in and out of the pupil is the target people should be looking for........and no one debunking this or supporting it realizes it.

At a macroscopic level iridology has poor to no anatomically evidence for changes in the iris pattern other than variations in pigmentation in the first year of life and variations caused by [glaucoma](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Glaucoma) treatment. The other problem is that detractors point to the use of the iris for identification. The stability of iris structures is the foundation of the [biometric](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biometric) technology which uses [iris recognition](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Iris_recognition) for identification purposes. What the debunkers fail to realize is that this software shines light from out to in.........and they find stability........but the pupil is a perfect black box radiator........that means light comes and and goes in both directions. So this means we dont know if there are any quantum changes to the bio-photons released from the eye. There is some data now that there is...........

CITES:

\[1\] Herbert Schwabl, Herbert Klima. **[Spontaneous ultraweak photon emission from biological systems and the endogenous light field.](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/living-systems-give-measurable-intensities-light-ie-photons-visible-part-electromagnetic)** Forsch Komplementarmed Klass Naturheilkd. 2005 Apr;12(2):84-9. PMID: [15947466](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/living-systems-give-measurable-intensities-light-ie-photons-visible-part-electromagnetic)

[\[2\]](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/biophotons-human-body-emits-communicates-and-made-light?page=1#_ftnref2) Hugo J Niggli, Salvatore Tudisco, Giuseppe Privitera, Lee Ann Applegate, Agata Scordino, Franco Musumeci. **[Laser-ultraviolet-A-induced ultraweak photon emission in mammalian cells.](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/laser-ultraviolet-induced-ultraweak-photon-emission-mammalian-cells)** J Biomed Opt. 2005 Mar-Apr;10(2):024006. PMID: [15910080](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/laser-ultraviolet-induced-ultraweak-photon-emission-mammalian-cells)

[\[3\]](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/biophotons-human-body-emits-communicates-and-made-light?page=1#_ftnref3) Chao Wang, István Bókkon, Jiapei Dai, István Antal. **[Spontaneous and visible light-induced ultraweak photon emission from rat eyes.](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/spontaneous-and-visible-light-induced-ultraweak-photon-emission-rat-eyes)** Brain Res. 2011 Jan 19 ;1369:1-9. Epub 2010 Oct 26. PMID: [21034725](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/spontaneous-and-visible-light-induced-ultraweak-photon-emission-rat-eyes)

[\[4\]](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/blog/biophotons-human-body-emits-communicates-and-made-light?page=1#_ftnref4) I Bókkon, R L P Vimal, C Wang, J Dai, V Salari, F Grass, I Antal. **[Visible light induced ocular delayed bioluminescence as a possible origin of negative afterimage.](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/visible-light-induced-ocular-delayed-bioluminescence-possible-origin-negative-afterimage)** J Photochem Photobiol B. 2011 May 3 ;103(2):192-9. Epub 2011 Mar 23. PMID: [21463953](http://www.greenmedinfo.com/article/visible-light-induced-ocular-delayed-bioluminescence-possible-origin-negative-afterimage)

### lohd2015 — 2016-03-28T00:38:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191604>

I was looking at my eyes in the mirror yesterday and noticed the color is much lighter than a few months ago before I started sungazing. The color of my eyes have always been dark brown and it was difficult to see my pupils. Yesterday, I was able to see everything very clearly, as the iris is now a light brown color.

I have a question: do you know why as we age, there is a thin white ring around the inside of the iris? The ring seems to be more noticeable with the lightening of my iris now.
Thank you all.

### Sue-UK — 2016-03-28T11:28:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/melanin-dopamine-iridology.17733/post-191634>

> I was looking at my eyes in the mirror yesterday and noticed the color is much lighter than a few months ago before I started sungazing. The color of my eyes have always been dark brown and it was difficult to see my pupils. Yesterday, I was able to see everything very clearly, as the iris is now a light brown color.
>
> I have a question: do you know why as we age, there is a thin white ring around the inside of the iris? The ring seems to be more noticeable with the lightening of my iris now.
> Thank you all.

It will be interesting to see if they lighten more as time goes on! Can you see the fibres of the iris? With an iridology torch or an iridology photo it's possible to see them more easily, unless they have pigment deposits layered right over. As pigment layers go, it then often reveals hidden signs. But pigment is interesting. If each different pigment absorbs wavelengths of a specific frequency, (from the outside in) or releases (emits, excretes...?) specific wavelengths of light from the inside out, then maybe the sulphur in the GI tract section of the iris that HopingMom mentions is serving a purpose until the environment changes.

# Thread: Hello there ftom NZ
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-there-ftom-nz.17738/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Kelli Booth — 2016-03-27T14:42:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-there-ftom-nz.17738/post-191580>

Hi I'm Kelli from Riverhead Auckland New Zealand. Just watched a few of Doc Jack's You Tube posts 'Wow' very interesting. Recommended by my accountant of all people.
Had Breast Cancer 3 years ago, surgery, chemo, radiotherapy. I think my cancer was from a huge amount of stress and fear that I suffered from a bad relationship situation where I nearly lost everything I had. Additionally my mother has borderline personality disorder and as children we were badly physically and emotionally abused growing up. I grew up wondering if I was going to die from the beatings I got and then developed panic disorder in my late teens.
After my cancer diagnosis, on advice of my surgeon I been doing LCHF for the past 10 months and been researching lots of different health opinions. I have lost 13 kgs and my joint pain and inflammation from the Letrozole I am on has improved immeasurably. My breast cancer was hormone positive both estro and progesterone and I was lymph node positive (3) ) Doc Jack makes a lot of sense. Just want to optimise my health and create an environment that gives me the best life I can have; after all it's pretty precious. How you are, how you make a positive difference to others and leaving this planet a better place is important to me.

I live in a small rural village. Our water supply is rain water that is stored in a concrete tank that is buried in the ground. We have wi fi and TV but limited use. I started my own Montessori Preschool 10 years ago. It is very holistic, we operate from a place of love and nurture. The school is on half an acre of mature trees. The kids look after our organic gardens, collect rain water, climb trees, wear bare feet, make compost, mow the lawns, play music, make art, love learning and truly respect eachother and their environment. I love coming to work. We do have wireless through out the school for our record keeping which I am now worried about. The children have minimal screen time only for projects maybe 15 mins per week.
Anyway just wanted to say hi and thank you Dr. Jack for your amazing work and having the balls to say it how it is.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-03-28T04:16:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hello-there-ftom-nz.17738/post-191615>
