### Jack Kruse — 2016-06-16T01:50:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-webinar-meets-sulfur-and-selenium.17861/post-196654>

All thermodynamic relationships depend on scale. This is a deep lesson of the April webinar if you have not figured this out. If you double the sides of a box, then you get four times the area, but you get eight times the volume. Volume factors scale much faster than area factors in scaling. Now think about these factors when we go from a large scale to that of 30-50 Angstroms in your inner mitochondrial membrane where the cristae are as water is heated up to shrink the respiratory proteins. Think about the June 2016 webinar. An elephant faces an entirely different set of problems than a mouse, based entirely on the relative effects of scale. And a mitochondria takes full advantage of scale as it shrinks because of QED.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-08-11T20:28:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-webinar-meets-sulfur-and-selenium.17861/post-199520>

So with the August 2016 Q & A coming up I thought if you like Frauhofer lines and how they linked to mitochondria you might like this link too since you've already been exposed to the April 2016 webinar. Might neutrino's affect elements on Earth via molecular resonant energy transfers? If so could neutrino's effect us the same way? April's radical idea was how could a neutrino effect a mitochondria was it not? What this paper suggests is the exact same thing but with a twist. They believe now that something that doesn't really interact with anything is changing something that can't be changed. I chuckled when I read it. Welcome to the party. I love when sceince catches up to crazy. THE STRANGE CASE OF FLARES AND RADIOACTIVE ELEMENTS
It's a mystery that presented itself unexpectedly: The radioactive decay of some elements sitting quietly in laboratories on Earth seemed to be influenced by activities inside the sun, 93 million miles away.
Is this possible?
Researchers from Stanford and Purdue University believe it is. But their explanation of how it happens opens the door to yet another mystery.
There is even an outside chance that this unexpected effect is brought about by a previously unknown particle emitted by the sun. "That would be truly remarkable," said Peter Sturrock, Stanford professor emeritus of applied physics and an expert on the inner workings of the sun. [http://news.stanford.edu/news/2010/august/sun-082310.html](http://news.stanford.edu/news/2010/august/sun-082310.html)

### Jack Kruse — 2016-12-03T15:23:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/april-webinar-meets-sulfur-and-selenium.17861/post-204706>

So members you thought that epic April 2016 webinar on the end game of mitochondria was intense? Well this relaity might be more ground breaking to you: Now we have others going down the same path as I did ten years ago when I came up my my thesis that a mitochodria is capable of nuclear reactions using neutrinos. If I am correct in my speculation based upon my data collection and research we should find proof that elements below atomic number 26 can under go transmutation in cells (because of what occurs in mito) obviating the need for many raw materials of atoms for key enzymes and proteins from food. This means what is today believed to be essential no longer is. In other words: sunlight can be turned directly into matter based upon what the cell needs when it needs it. Lavoisier has established a mass conservation law which is valid in chemistry. Now we know that it is NOT true when nuclear reactions are involved. The review of more than two centuries of research demonstrates that this is not true in biology. It appears that all living organisms can under some circumstances produce nuclear reactions. However, there is an important need of finding an adequate theory to explain these results. It is highly probable that such a theory should also be capable of explaining Cold Fusion, or more generally, nuclear reactions in condensed matter. Another point is the irreproducibility of some experiments. Probably, in order to produce significant transmutation of an element, it is necessary that another element be missing. It seems that nature has a tendency to find ways to transmute an element into another to provide the necessary ingredients for the healthy growth of the four kingdoms of bacteria, fungi, plants and animals, including human beings. [http://www.e-catworld.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/09/JCMNS-Biberian.pdf](http://www.e-catworld.com/wp-content/uploads/2015/09/JCMNS-Biberian.pdf) It appears this picture needs to be updated for mitochondria now.

# Thread: Making a Kerrygold Butter run...Canadian style
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-a-kerrygold-butter-run-canadian-style.17862/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Rubicon — 2016-04-25T19:13:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/making-a-kerrygold-butter-run-canadian-style.17862/post-193457>

I really like Kerrygold Butter. I could eat around a stick a day if I had easy access to it. Unfortunately Kerrygold is unavailable in Canada (the local dairy industry is very protective), and there are no ideal substitutes that have the same quality and affordability.

As a result, anytime I go to the US, I load up my suitcase with Kerrygold and cross my fingers that the customs agents are in a good mood. Canadians can bring 20lbs of butter, but anything over a few sticks may be subject to high ~300% tariffs. A recent trip back from Hawaii worked out great. 70 sticks of unsalted, and 10 sticks of salted Kerrygold!

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

Whole Foods had unsalted on the east side of Oahu. A few hours before my flight I went to Whole Foods, transferred the butter to one of their freezers (I stashed it in with the frozen vegetables), and returned a few hours later to purchase it. When checking out, the clerk was pretty amazed but mentioned that it wasn't the first time she'd seen someone buying such a large quantity. Perhaps I'm not the first Canadian to make a mass purchase of Kerrygold...

I packed a few ziplock bags of ice into the luggage to help cool it and all the butter made it back to Canada nice and cold 14 hours later. Luckily, no questions asked by customs.

### Jenelle — 2016-04-25T21:34:47+0300

### shah78 — 2016-04-25T22:00:27+0300

### Rubicon — 2016-04-25T22:31:19+0300

Unfortunately they were $4.69 per stick in Hawaii...a little more than the usual ~$3 per stick in California.

There's actually a sortof black market here where people occasionally sell the stuff for $20 per stick. If I'm able to build up enough of a cache before winter, I might try selling a bit and see if I can make enough to pay for my flight. Lol

### Inger — 2016-04-26T13:33:54+0300

Oh.. that is expensive! I get kerrygold butter here regular price 1,70 euro/stick
and often they are on sale for 1 euro/stick......
But I do better with raw organic grassfed butter..... which is more expensive tho so kerrygold is my choice if I need butter and I have to save money

I buy my raw butter from here (Austria), when I can spend more money....

[https://schaetzeausoesterreich.at/rohmilchbutter](https://schaetzeausoesterreich.at/rohmilchbutter)

but it is 20 euro / 1 kg! But excellent quality... like a dream.....

I had a difficult time with butter until I started taking Betaine HCL. Now I seem to be able to digest it reasonably well.

### thomas — 2016-04-26T21:16:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-would-one-hook-up-an-ipad-to-the-ethernet-connection.17863/post-193591>

### lohd2015 — 2016-04-26T21:34:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-would-one-hook-up-an-ipad-to-the-ethernet-connection.17863/post-193598>

> personally I think a laptop is better.(you can chose among 1000 of models the least worst and customise individual parts)
> if ipad doesn't have an ethernet port then use an usb/ethernet converter.

It seems that most of the usb-ethernet converters available on Amazon states that your device has to support Ethernet, otherwise the converters won't work. I followed the instructions on one of them to check out if I can use it for my Android tablet. Unfortunately, I do not have the 'Ethernet' option in the settings under "Wireless and Networks".

If your device supports Ethernet, then it would be listed there, but the option would be 'grayed out'.

# Thread: CALLING ALL STARFISH!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-starfish.17866/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Curves — 2016-04-27T01:50:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-starfish.17866/post-193628>

A few weeks ago I posted a thank you to Dr. Jack Kruse for being the catalyst to my rebirth in the Quantum Health FB Group.
The number of people who shared their own stories and thanks to Dr. Kruse was profound.
That gave me an idea.
Now, I know there are a lot of mixed feelings about technology in this community but for anyone who'd like to share their story live, I thought it might be fun to host a live Blab session.
If you're unfamiliar with Blab it's a live streaming format that allows up to four people to video stream at the same time, while an unlimited number of people can attend in the audience. You can check it out here at [https://blab.im/](https://blab.im/).
The format of this particular Blab won't be super sciencey but rather a place where we can share our stories and biohacks in a supportive friendly environment, and learn from each other.
If this resonates with you and you'd like to share your story live or just hang out in the audience, please let me know in the comments or direct message me.
I'll post the date of my first Blab shortly.
In love and appreciation for this community,
Elaine

### caroline — 2016-04-27T03:15:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-starfish.17866/post-193639>

Great idea Elaine! I have been thinking something similar lately.....

Dr. K. gets so frustrated with us because we miss important stuff sometimes.

I am finding over and over in conversations I have with others here ......It is easier to see the gaps in what someone else is doing/or not doing/missing - than for ourselves.

That is what Dr. K. sees clearly - all our weak spots that we aren't seeing for ourselves - for lots of reasons I think.
Everyone is so busy with life. There are kids to take care of, partners to support, a job to go to, bills to pay, keep the house clean, do the laundry, cooking......how endless is this list????

Dr. K. has support staff in his clinic, in his surgery and at home..... he can concentrate on what he does best.

I have no problem with seeing the forest .... but I do have a problem with missing the trees. Is anyone else like that!

I do get the overall picture ....but often can't get out of my own way to make necessary adjustments ....unless someone \[Dr. K.\] clubs me over the head! often by saying "the lights are on - but is anyone home?

### Rubicon — 2016-04-27T03:26:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calling-all-starfish.17866/post-193640>

I'm interested in joining the audience. The forum is great, but real-time spoken communication has certain advantages.

# Thread: Action Time
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/action-time.17869/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### JBS — 2016-04-27T07:14:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/action-time.17869/post-193669>

G’day Team Kruse

OK it’s time to bare all, seems naked truth is what this forum is built on. Don’t worry no photo’s

Age: 49
Divorced with one 15 y/o step-daughter
Live: Manly, Sydney Australia – top (3rd) floor apartment overlooking Sydney Harbour through pine trees, surf beach close by.
Work: Serial entrepreneur. Until very recently spent my working life in an 1st floor office in the CBD under fluro lights starring at a computer most of the day.
Quals: Bachelor and Master’s Degrees in Env Science (8 years at uni studying environmental health with sun/blue light, magnetism and nnEMF barely mentioned).

The problem – blue light disease, not enough DHA and connection to Big Mamma/Papa
Have had weight issues for 30 years all diet nor exercise regimes had no lasting effects. Bloods taken in mid Feb and confirmed what would happen to someone with my lifestyle i.e. pre-diabetic, low testosterone and metabolic syndrome. I have been blessed with a highly resilient body and amazed it has lasted this long given the abuse.

**Remedial Action**
Stay away from main stream medicine and fix my environment.
Started Leptin Rx late Feb 2016 (good results to date and will report after 12 weeks, 6-8 weeks won’t be enough for the damage I have done to my body).
Daily morning sun and no sunglasses ever.
Drink spring water or sparkling mineral water.
There is no way can I do CT as recommended but instead go the beach every morning and snorkel or body surf for 30-60 mins (sea temp is currently 20-22C but decreasing now we are going into winter to around 15-16C) and plan to continue fun CT during winter.
Reduced my nnEMF by working from home mostly with balcony doors open wide to natural light, purchased a Blushield device and cable connected my gadgets.
Added f.lux to screens and wear blue blockers at night if watching any screen. Lights are off.
Only grounding I am getting is time at the beach.

**Goals**
Optimal health (lose 30+kg, no metabolic syndrome, etc.)
Write a book on sun/light and nnEMF to help educate people
Start a new biophysics tech company

Cheers
Jason

### JBS — 2016-08-03T05:52:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/action-time.17869/post-199242>

Update

Fell off the Leptin Rx wagon over June/July (winter downunder), nothing too serious but rice with Asian food, chips with fish, sandwiches, a few boozy boys nights out and some deserts with my daughter. Little or no ocean swimming but ate loads of seafood. Stayed true to blue light mitigation and had around a 30% morning sun hit rate. Sunglasses remained in the draw. Interestingly I have not gained any weight (3 notches off my belt)! But i did not lose anymore either. Energy levels and libido improved, blood pressure dropped a bit but remains very high and have not checked my bloods. So back on the wagon this month. My suspicion is that blue light is a major culprit in my n=1 journey. Been trailing blushield (photonic micro crystal tech) using plants for my experiments re nnEMF with surprising success.

My new book _In The Dark - new ways to avoid the harmful effects of living in a technologically connected world_ is with the publisher they are dumbing it down against my wishes but better to get the message out in a format mothers can understand. The only way we are going to educate the masses about the profound "back to nature" knowledge is by building an army of angry mothers! Pissed off mothers are the only population group politicians and the establishment are afraid of.

### JBS — 2016-10-30T03:49:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/action-time.17869/post-203441>

Been back on Leptin RX (with some CT) for 4 weeks again and the weight just falls off. A far way to go but down from 145kg to 129kg is pretty darn good! On top of that my knees have almost fully recovered from Grade IV cartilage damage - tennis court awaits!

When I got hit by the quantum health wave, especially circadian biology and Wallace’s work on mitochondria, my life changed. It brought me back into the environmental health game which I left in revulsion 25 years ago. Invigorated and inspired I have decided to go on the front foot and share my take in the most effective way I know how. My new book In The Dark is being launched this week and I am doing interviews and podcasts to help spread the message. Written for non-science degree mums and dads who want the best health and well-being for themselves and their children while living in a technologically addicted world. If Pollacks book is for 3rd graders this for kindergarten kids. Those 'ignorant idiots' that Jack refers to include my family and colleagues and they need to be fed like babies if they are going to take quantum health seriously.

# Thread: my journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journal.17870/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hello — 2016-04-27T14:53:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journal.17870/post-193688>

Hello all, Laney here. Thanks for posting such great stuff on this forum.

This is mostly a placeholder until I can get round to writing more, so I thought I'd start by writing up snippets or observations.

For a bit over a year I've had trouble breathing, an undiagnosable condition (at least undiagnosable by the doctors I saw). I found that sitting in the sun while grounding, or lacking sun just grounding helps my breathing basically immediately, and if I stay out in full blast sun long enough the effect will last a good couple of days.

I found this forum and after starting to do some reading on here and combined with my own personal experience it seems to me that there are more important interventions to make in terms of getting healthy than supplements (and I spent months taking \*bucketloads\* of them with little effect until I tried my sun experiment).

The only other things I've found that has had an effect on my breathing trouble has been magnesium put on transdermally.

I'm homozygous for MTHFR A1298c, and I see from some other posts here that one gets by this SNP by moving to the equator. I'm in Sydney and I know sunlight exposure here is perhaps a bad thing - ozone hole? - but, if so, I have to take a risk there because of my strong preference for being able to breathe.

I do have a reptile lamp from rayminder.org I've been thinking about using, but don't think I understand enough yet about the technology to start off.

Thanks for listening!

ps: I'm a Sydneysider, get in touch if yo want to compare notes re: sunbaking, water, etc.

### Jenelle — 2016-04-28T21:12:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-journal.17870/post-193785>

"my strong preference for being able to breathe"

Yeah... Can't argue with that!

Welcome.

# Thread: grounding mats
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mats.17871/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### hello — 2016-04-27T14:57:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mats.17871/post-193690>

Hello all,

I was just watching this youtube:

It shows a guy whose voltage goes way down when he is on a grounding mat near his computer gear. He's making the point that you need one when you are working on a computer or surrounded by EMF.

I decided not to get a grounding mat because I read here and elsewhere that it makes you an antenna for EMF.

What does it mean to be an 'antenna' for EMF? If the guy in the video above - if his voltage in his body is going low, does that mean his body isn't acting as an antenna in this instance?

I normally ground for about an hour outside each day and preferably in the sun but I'd love to ground for longer if I can find a way. is there something on this I should be reading?

any thoughts let me know!

### caroline — 2016-05-01T08:16:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mats.17871/post-193930>

Grounding mats are kind of iffy ..... When you plug it in you could be getting dirty electricity from the outlet.

I am sure lots of folks here have hacked this for themselves.

You need to consider a lot of things .....like overhead power lines and towers near you and smart meters....

### jwhb77 — 2016-05-01T18:14:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-mats.17871/post-193954>

As Caroline said, there are a number of factors to consider... be careful, pay attention to your experiences, & think things through before experimenting...

Personally, I'd never use an electrical outlet to ground an earthing mat or blanket... much better to run a covered wire outside & ground it to a copper grounding rod that is planted 10 or more feet deep into the earth. This avoids the dirty electricity that can be lurking in your home's wiring, as well as any transient currents near the earth's surface that could get transmitted via a shallower grounding rod. Most big-box home improvement-type stores carry these kinds of rods. Note: You might need some 'muscle' to help you drive it into the ground, though, depending on the composition of your soil.

If you want to take more extensive precautions, you could also build a mini Faraday cage over the exposed end of the grounding rod (though not touching it), to prevent the possibility of any antenna-like reception & transmission of ambient currents. This is probably an extreme measure, but could be relevant depending on your local EMF conditions.

Another possibility is using a body voltage meter to test your setup & its various configurations.

Above all, whatever you do or don't do, I recommend keeping a detailed, running log of what you've done & the effects that any or all of these measures have on you personally. Generally speaking, if you're not testing, you're just guessing. Hope this helps

# Thread: Which light bulb looks most like sunlight?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-light-bulb-looks-most-like-sunlight.17872/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Optimalbound — 2016-04-27T19:01:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-light-bulb-looks-most-like-sunlight.17872/post-193702>

This video compares the spectrum from incandescent, fluorescent and two different kinds of LED lights. No halogen lights, unfortunately, but informative nonetheless. It also shows how badly some LED lights pulsate with the a/c frequency.

The peaks in the blue parts of the spectrum from LEDs and fluorescent lights are clearly visible, but they seem a bit smaller than I have seen in other analyses, for some reason. My understanding of the Bluetech lenses is that they narrowly filter out most of these peaks but leave the rest of the blue largely intact, which is why they appear only slightly tinted, and which is why they make sense to wear in artificial lighting during daytime but don't really replace blueblockers for use after sunset.

### Curves — 2016-04-27T19:19:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/which-light-bulb-looks-most-like-sunlight.17872/post-193706>

> [Optimalbound said:](/goto/post?id=193702)

> This video compares the spectrum from incandescent, fluorescent and two different kinds of LED lights. No halogen lights, unfortunately, but informative nonetheless. It also shows how badly some LED lights pulsate with the a/c frequency.
>
> The peaks in the blue parts of the spectrum from LEDs and fluorescent lights are clearly visible, but they seem a bit smaller than I have seen in other analyses, for some reason. My understanding of the Bluetech lenses is that they narrowly filter out most of these peaks but leave the rest of the blue largely intact, which is why they appear only slightly tinted, and which is why they make sense to wear in artificial lighting during daytime but don't really replace blueblockers for use after sunset.
>

The slowmo pulsing light finale at the end was truly eye opening to watch. Very dramatic.
What do you make of the low spike in blue light in the LED bulbs? From his analysis it seems like only fluorescent bulbs fail to mimic natural sunlight.
Thanks for posting this!

# Thread: Reflecting on my health over the years
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reflecting-on-my-health-over-the-years.17873/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### pascale ahmad — 2016-04-28T13:32:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reflecting-on-my-health-over-the-years.17873/post-193753>

As I will be turning 50 in a few months, I have been reflecting on the state of my health over the years and how I now find myself in perimenopause, 25kg overweight, leptin resistant, insulin, having digestive issues and chronically constipated for at leat 20 years and feeling generally quite apathetic.
I shall start at the beginning.
I know I was not breastfed as a baby, neither was my mother, I am not sure what the reason was. Apparently I was "difficult" baby who hardly ever slept and cried incessantly for most of the first year of my life.
I grew up in a very healthy rural environment, no polution, no EMF. We ate a very clean diet of grass fed beef, vegetables from our garden and unpasteurised cheeses. Other than the usual childhood illnesses like measles, whooping cough, mums and chicken pox (which in those days children were expected to contract and be done with it, and not vaccinated against) I was a healthy child. I was not given antibiotics and the likes as my mother was more inclined towards homeopathy and herbal remedies.
I think I was probably showing early signs of digestive issues because I remember my mother often giving me charcoal.
At the age of 17 I left my native Burgundy and moved to Paris to go design school. Shortly afterwards I was "discovered" by a model agency scout and started a modelling carrier. From then on I lived what I could only describe as a "rock n roll" lifestyle in blue light. I was either in the studio in flash light or on runways and permanently jet lagged and sleep deprived. I paid no attention to what I was eating and smoked a pack a day. I had no weight problems.
By my mid twenties, living in London by then, I began to suffer from chronic digestive issues, painful digestion, bloating and constipation. I saw doctors who invariably would tell me there was nothing wrong with me and I should eat more fibre or that i had IBS (whatever that means!). I tried a candida diets, colonic irrigation, colon cleanse. You name it , I tried it....to no avail.
By my late twenties I was no longer modelling, I had gone back to designing clothes but I was still "living it up". By now it was getting more and more difficult to remain slim, I was a "super cool fashion babe" and it was absolutely unthinkable for me to be fat so I indulged in diet pills to maintain my weight.
In my 30's I started to get a lot of migraines, I had always been prone to headaches even as a child. At had my son at 36, it was an easy pregnancy and natural delivery but I piled on 20kg which would not come off. I breast fed my son for a year (the theory that I breastfeeding makes you lose pregnancy weight is a load of bollocks!) and then went back to diet pills to lose the weight.
A years later as we were getting ready to moved to India, I found out I was pregnant again, I miscarried in India a couple of months later. I was never able to get pregnant again after that, having only one child is my biggest regret in life.
The first 7 years of our life in India were extremely tough, not only for me to adapt to a very different culture but also because we were faced with an onslaught of difficulties and obstacles which resulted in constant worry, anxiety and sleepless nights. I think I was probably verging on depression even tough at the time I refused to give into it. I had migraines all the time.
We did weather the storm eventually but I feel that all these years of stress really took their toll on me. When it was all over, I was in perimenopause and my weight has been totally out of control for the past 3 years (I have stopped taking diet pills more than 10 years ago).
I take full responsibility for my health, I know that I abused my body in the past. I am ready and fully committed to getting healthy and can only hope that the damage is not irreparable.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-04-28T14:41:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reflecting-on-my-health-over-the-years.17873/post-193757>

The stress built in to this journey is clear. That stress likely drove your results. Where change begins and ends is indeterminate. The way we sense change is looking at the environment around us and notice things are new. Our cells respond to these environmental changes by expanding or contracting the respiratory proteins in our mitochondria. Your story seems to be one of expansion alone, in your mitochondria. You need to watch Doug Wallace's video I have posted many times in the Time series. Pay attention to the part at 50:00 min on ward.

### Jack Kruse — 2016-04-29T21:46:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-15-now-live.17874/post-193850>

Salinity is related to charge. The ocean and your cells are like because of these physical relationships.
Salinity is linked to EZ says Time 15 . FYI: got to understand how plasma works: FEW DO

[https://podaac.jpl.nasa.gov/SeaSurfaceSalinity](https://podaac.jpl.nasa.gov/SeaSurfaceSalinity)

Sea water is the ultimate plasma of life. It is also why every cell has salty water inside of a cell. If the sun shines down on the water and some of it evaporates, the salt is left behind and the water will become saltier. Similarly, if some fresh water is added, such as from rain or melting ice, the salt will be diluted and the water will be less salty.

Salinity does change with depth, but the way it changes depends very much on where one happens to be. That's because of another property of water, its density. Density is how "heavy" a parcel of water is. And "heavier" water tends to sink and stay below "lighter" water (have a look at this answer from our archives for a little more on how that works). The density of seawater depends mostly on how much salt is dissolved in it (more dissolved salt = "heavier" seawater), but temperature also has an effect. The abrupt change in temperature at a thermocline occurs because the density of water changes as a function of temperature. Pure water, sans salt, has maximum density at around 4 degrees C (3.98 if you want to be fussy). Of course, in a lake, the most dense water will be found at the bottom, with less dense water overlying it; this point was expanded in Ubiquitination 5 to a great degree as it relates to Rayleigh Benard convection. That is the lowest energy state, which is the way the physical world generally likes to be. This is why energy in water always flows or cycles naturally without any work needed by a cell. A cell takes advantage of this physical property of water to innovate life.

Now then, if you imagine the sun shining down on a lake, the water at the surface will of course begin to warm and change the surface temperature in relation to the deeper cooler water. This sets up the convection cell naturally I mentioned above. That will make that water less dense (thus more buoyant), and it will therefore float over the cooler water below it. The cooler water below has more electron density because it is more dense and the surface water has less electron density in it. The hydrogen bonding networks in both densities of water are also different and so is now they work with the photoelectric effect. This tells you their charge and size of their EZ will differ too. This is the critical physical part a mitochondria is built to sense inside of our cells. In the ocean or lake it is important to the ecosystem and all things that live in it. Why? Because the warmer the temperature of the water becomes the more it EXPANDS and loses density and gains buoyancy. This is why it will float on deeper cooler water. Energy in water columns flows from cool to hot because of convection. It turns out, it is not easy to move heat downward in the water column (pelagic water), and that is what causes a very sharp thermocline we see in oceans especially in the poles and Gulf. In practice, be sure there is a small amount of heat conduction by the water, as well as mixing by the surface winds, and that will cause some LOCAL "thickening" of the thermoclines when we measure them in lakes. Remember lakes and oceans are difference. The Gulf is different from the ocean too because of salinity.
Essentially the same thing happens in the open oceans, but there is the added complication of salt in the water (which also has an effect on the density of seawater), and very substantial wind mixing that tends to make oceanic thermoclines a little less distinct than we see in lakes, rivers, and reservoirs. So, if water has more salt in it, it will tend to be "heavy" and will tend to sink. And often there is an increase in salinity with an increase in depth in polar waters. Life there takes full advantage of this. Life at the surface of the sea takes advantage of its local environment and your mitochondria takes advantage of this version of plasma in your cells in similar fashion because salinity relates to charge and to energy flows. All these little details your food guru's are clueless of.

### nonchalant — 2016-04-30T17:44:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-you-blinded-by-the-modern-worlds-version-of-light.17875/post-193892>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=193776)
>
> [http://www.gizmag.com/fourth-state-of-water/42999/](http://www.gizmag.com/fourth-state-of-water/42999/)

> The water under question was found in super-small six-sided channels in the mineral beryl, which forms the basis for the gems aquamarine and emerald. The channels measure only about five atoms across and function basically as cages that can each trap one water molecule.

Sounds like our porphyrin ring friends heme and chlorophyll, but with silicates pinching water. We know water traps UV, it doesn't have to be a heavy metal at the center. And it's much more interesting.

No wonder we like aquamarine & emeralds. Makes me wonder if there is water trapped in my humble little quartz geode. It has a ring structure too.

Aquamarine... Is it just me, or do our words and idioms hide some profound truths that have been mostly forgotten?

> The group identified and quantified hundreds of mitochondrial proteins, finding that the quantities of a whopping 40% peak once a day. Further research identified the proteins making up the mitochondrial circadian clock that regulates these activities. Surprisingly, most of the circadian proteins in the mitochondria peaked four hours into the daylight part of the cycle (in mice, which are active at night).

Surprisingly?? I know this is about mice, but it makes sense that humans would have increased synthesis of mitochondrial proteins at night. We all know that the ETC is really screaming fast at night, with those electrons hurtling themselves towards oxygen like crazy, making tons of energy for repair & maintenance. Melatonin, baby!

### nonchalant — 2016-04-30T20:50:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-you-blinded-by-the-modern-worlds-version-of-light.17875/post-193903>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=193774)
>
> [http://phys.org/news/2016-04-superfast-source-artificial-atom.html](http://phys.org/news/2016-04-superfast-source-artificial-atom.html)

> Petru Tighineanu, a postdoc in the Quantum Photonics research group at the Niels Bohr Institute, has carried out the experiments and he explains the effect as such, that the atoms are very small and light is very 'big' because of its long wavelength, so the light almost cannot 'see' the atoms – like a lorry that is driving on a road and does not notice a small pebble. But if many pebbles become a larger stone, the lorry will be able to register it and then the interaction becomes much more dramatic. In the same way, light interacts much more strongly with the quantum dot if the quantum dot contains the special superradiant quantum state, which makes it look much bigger.

It's interesting to think of our mitochondria housing millions of these potential quantum dots. And when those dots are compressed and organize, they become important in light/matter interaction and release superfast light.

### Sue-UK — 2016-05-02T17:51:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-you-blinded-by-the-modern-worlds-version-of-light.17875/post-194020>

I read of an experiment done years ago (when measuring single cell voltages was probably more difficult than now) whereby they took brown seaweed egg cells (Focus) and lined them up in a thin tube with electrodes at either end. Then shone light down one end to try and get them to grow. Long before they could, the cells polarised, positive and negative ends but all they same way, with the positive end nearest the light, so that the tiny voltages in each added up along the length of the tube, like a battery series, producing an easily measurable current.

I was reading about cultures that chose a dot to represent the concept of 'nothing' or 'empty' and that a hollow dot stood for 'something" or "nothing as something" or as a positional notation, such as the 0's position in 120 or 102. So I have been musing about sunlight hitting quantum dots turning dots into hollow dots because the electrons make holes..... Off outside to supercharge my quantum dots
